file.content:
Mental imagery, the ability to form internal visual representations of the external world, is thought to play an essential role in a number of cognitive processes, including episodic memory, visual working memory, and spatial navigation. Astoundingly, recent work describes individuals who are unable to voluntarily form mental images – a condition that has been recently dubbed aphantasia. Despite their lack of mental imagery, these individuals are able to live normal lives and have been shown to be capable at visual working memory tasks, albeit employing non-visual strategies. Further work suggests that their imagery lack sensory visual properties altogether, and it remains an open question of how this affects their visual processing. Here we aim to assess if individuals with aphantasia are able internally supplement missing visual details through the process of pattern completion and how vulnerable this process is to visual disruption. We recruited thirty-four individuals with aphantasia to complete a back-masking task in which we briefly presented participants with partially occluded images and asked them to categorize the shown object into one of four categories. Crucially, these partial images were followed by a visual noise mask, aimed at disrupting recurrent processing that is thought to support pattern separation. We initially expected to find that individuals with aphantasia would be undisrupted by backwards masking, instead finding that they were more impacted by backwards masking relative to controls. This suggests they have intact, but poor, recurrent processing and are thus more susceptible to visual interference.
Second draft (Currently over word limit: 256/250)

Though mental imagery is thought to play an essential role in human cognition, there are people who are unable to form voluntary images and yet have no obvious cognitive deficits—a condition that has been called aphantasia. We hypothesize that aphantasia may involve a tendency to operate on perceptual representations at higher levels of abstraction, coupled with a lack of high-to-low level feedback processes, which are thought to be important for vivid visual imagery. In this work we attempt to isolate such recurrent processing using a perceptual pattern completion task with a backward masking manipulation.  Perceptual pattern completion is thought to be supported by recurrent processing whereby activation from a partial cue spreads to missing features. This recurrent processing can be inhibited through backward masking. But since individuals with aphantasia may avoid—or have a deficit with respect to—such recurrent processing, is their performance differentially impacted by visual backward masking? We recruited thirty-four individuals with aphantasia to complete a backward-masking task in which we briefly presented participants with partially occluded images and asked them to categorize the shown object into one of four categories. Crucially, these partial images were followed by a visual noise mask, aimed at disrupting recurrent processing that is thought to support pattern completion. We initially expected to find that individuals with aphantasia would be undisrupted by backwards masking, instead finding that they were more impacted by backwards masking relative to controls. This suggests they have intact, but poor, recurrent processing and are thus more susceptible to visual interference.
Aphantasia Study

Literature Review
When asked to picture a summer’s day on the beach, with cloudless blue skies and crashing waves, many people claim to be able to “see” an image in their “mind’s eye”. This ability to visualize experiences in the mind’s eye is referred to as mental imagery. Mental imagery is thought to be related to a number of cognitive activities, including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).
However, whether or not mental imagery has functional importance for cognition has been the subject of a long-standing debate (see Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). One way to gain insight into the relationship between mental imagery and cognitive processes is to investigate how individual differences in mental imagery abilities relate to more direct indices of cognitive performance. To gauge imagery strength, researchers have taken to collecting trial-by-trial self-reports of vividness or alternatively have used tools such as the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023) to quantify trait differences in mental imagery. Trial-to-trial variation in self-reported vividness ratings have been found to correlate with the number of visual details reported in autobiographical memories (D’Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), the degree of neural reinstatement during memory recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015) and the degree of neural overlap between imagery and perception (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). In addition, VVIQ scores predict the degree to which an mental imagery induces priming during binocular rivalry tasks (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011); and the size of pupillary light response when imagining bright versus dim stimuli (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). Thus, there is ample evidence suggesting that self-reported measures of mental imagery strength relates to neural and behavioral measures of cognitive performance in memory and perception tasks.
Interestingly, these self-reports have revealed individuals who have little to no experience with mental images. Cases of a lack of imagery have been reported since the 19th century (Galton, 1880), a condition now referred to as Aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015). Aphantasia is relatively rare, with recent reports indicating a prevalence rate as low as 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to as high as 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia (henceforth referred to as aphantasics) often interpret the phrase “seeing something in your mind” as a metaphor, and are surprised to learn that others actually report experiencing something more literally image-like (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). On the surface, aphantasics lead normal lives, notwithstanding the evidence reviewed above suggesting that mental imagery plays a role in a number of cognitive activities. How might their lack of mental imagery affect their cognition? 
When interviewed about their autobiographical memory, aphantasics report fewer episodic and visual details, with this effect being more prominent when they are asked to simulate hypothetical future events (Alexei J. Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Alexei J. Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020) suggesting a connection to a relative phemonon referred to as “severely deficient autobiographical memory” (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018).  Aphantasics do, however, report having dreams, suggesting that they may retain unconscious forms of mental imagery (Zeman et al., 2020), although it has been reported that aphantasics dream less frequently than controls and that these dreams tend to be less vivid (Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020). While the occurrence of unconscious mental imagery might suggest that aphantasia results from a failure of metacognition, behavioural studies have shown that they neither exhibit imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018), nor a change in the pupillary light response when imagining stimuli of different brightness (Kay et al., 2022). These findings have been taken to suggest that their mental representations indeed lack sensory and phenomenal properties. Taken together, the above-reviewed research suggests that aphantasics tend to process visual information at higher levels of abstraction. Indeed, when performing visual working memory tasks, aphantasics do as well as controls, but report having used strategies  that make use of semantic labels (Keogh et al., 2021) rather than lower level sensory information.
What neurological mechanisms might underlie aphantasia? Neuroimaging studies have demonstrated that the contents of their imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) can be decoded in the early visual cortex, suggesting content specific information is present in early visual cortex representations of aphantasics. However, the content of imagery could not be cross-decoded with activity from perception (Meng et al., 2023) suggesting that the information that aphantasics store in these representations appears to be different than their representations formed during perception. Additionally when aphantasics were asked to imagine a stimulus at a specific location, neural activity was greater in the ipsilateral hemisphere whereas in controls activity was greater in the contralateral hemisphere (Meng et al., 2023). Together these findings suggest that the imagery related representations of aphantasics either contains less—or transformed—sensory information compared to controls.
Another functional neuroimaging report showed that aphantasics exhibit reduced resting state connectivity between prefrontal cortices and visual cortices and less left anterior parietal activity when asked to actively visualize faces and scenes (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). Because mental imagery is believed to be supported by the top-down activation of sensory areas, it is thought to rely more heavily on recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices compared to perception (Nadine Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). Thus, the reduced connectivity might partly underlie the aphantasic inability to  voluntarily create mental images.
Similar findings were observed in an fMRI study examining the autobiographical memory deficits of aphantasics (Monzel et al., 2023). When asked to retrieve an autobiographical memory, aphantasics were shown to have reduced activation in bilateral hippocampus, increased activation of the visual cortices, and reduced connectivity between hippocampus and visual cortices. This increased activation of visual cortices during memory retrieval was particularly interesting, and suggested that the deficits to autobiographical memory may be related to a reduced signal-to-noise ratio caused by the hyper activity of visual cortices (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023).
In this paper, we ask how perceptual pattern completion, another process theorized to depend on recurrent connections (Rolls, 2013), might be impacted in aphantasia. Perceptual pattern completion enables us to recognize a partially degraded or occluded object. Activity from the partial object is thought to spread to the missing features through both lateral and vertical recurrent connections (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), and this has been supported with evidence from computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). This recurrent processing can be inhibited by backward masking stimuli with visual noise, impairing recognition (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O’Reilly, 2012). When a partial stimulus is viewed, higher areas process the resulting feedforward signals, forming predictions about the identity of the stimulus and send top-down feedback signals to lower areas, strengthening and reinforcing neural responses in lower areas. However, when a mask quickly follows the stimulus as in backward masking, feedforward signals from the mask travel up the processing stream at the same time as the feedback prediction signals related to the previous stimulus. This mismatch between feedforward and feedback signals is thought to be the cause of the observed impairments to perception by backward masking (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).
How might pattern completion in aphantasics be affected by visual disruption via backward masking? Backward masking is believed to selectively disrupt recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000), and because aphantasics have reduced connectivity between frontoparietal and visual cortices (Milton et al., 2021), they might be more heavily impacted by disruption and thus show difficulty in recognizing a partial stimulus when it is presented briefly and followed by a mask. However, aphantasics tend to favour semantic strategies when completing visual tasks (Keogh et al., 2021) and their imagery related presentations appear to have less sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023). It therefore may be the case that aphantasics are able to perform a kind of high-level pattern completion by relying on the retrieval of semantic details when presented with a partial visual stimulus. Thus, an alternative hypothesis is that, due to aphantasics’ tendency to process stimuli at a higher level of abstraction (and therefore not rely on top-down inference of low-level details), backward masking might have a reduced impact on their ability to recognize the partial stimulus. In the present study, we test these alternative hypotheses, in a group of self-identified aphantasics and matched controls that completed a task where we briefly presented partially occluded images depicting objects and asked participants to identify the object as belonging to one of four categories. Crucially, these partially occluded images were sometimes followed by a visual noise mask, aimed at disrupting recurrent processing that is thought to support pattern completion. 

Methods
Participants
Thirty-five individuals (aged 18-77) with self-described aphantasia were recruited through pages on Reddit and Facebook. An additional four individuals were identified through their VVIQ scores during the collection of control data, and these individuals were included in the aphantasia group. All aphantasic individuals were required to report that they could never remember a time that they experienced mental imagery and that there was no injury that led to them becoming aphantasic. Four individuals recruited from Reddit were excluded from analysis due to excessive non-responding or performing at or below the level of chance. A control group consisting of 41 age matched individuals (aged 18-77) was recruited through the online recruitment platform Prolific (www.Prolific.com), Reddit, and Facebook (Prolific: 38, Reddit: 2, Facebook: 1). 
Questionnaires
All participants with the exception of 17 control participants completed the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire 2 (VVIQ2; Marks, 1973). The VVIQ2 consists of 32 items which asks participants to imagine eight scenarios (e.g., driving on the highway, a day at the beach) and rate the vividness of their mental images on a 5-point scale ranging from 1 (“No image at all, you only “know” that you are thinking of the object) to 5 (“Perfectly clear and as vivid as normal vision”). 	
Stimuli
Three-hundred and twenty images were collected from a Google image search consisting of grayscale images of objects belonging to one of four categories (clothing, food, toys, and tools; see Figure 1). Images were resized to be a maximum of 450 pixels (horizontal or vertical) while maintaining original aspect ratios.
Behavioural Procedure
Participants completed two blocks consisting of 160 trials of a backwards masking task (Figure 2) as described in (Tang et al., 2018). On each trial, participants were presented with an image and were asked to categorize the displayed object into one of four possible semantic groups (1: clothing, 2: food, 3: tools, 4: toys). Each trial began with a 500 ms fixation period. Participants were then briefly presented with the image of an object for a variable duration (25 ms, 50 ms, 75 ms, 100 ms, or 150 ms). Forty exemplars from each category of the object categories were presented in each block, with eight presented at each of the five levels of stimulus duration. The image was then followed by a either a visual noise mask (50% of trials) or a blank gray screen (50% of trials) for 500 ms. Trials concluded with a forced-choice screen requiring the subject to respond with their categorization of the object by pressing the numbers 1-4 on the keyboard (1: clothing, 2: food, 3: tools, 4: toys).
On 25% of the trials, the images were presented in unaltered fashion. On the remaining 75% of the trials, the images were rendered partially occluded by presenting visual features through six Gaussian bubbles (Gosselin & Schyns, 2001) with a sigma value of 26 pixels. The number of bubbles (but not their positions) was then kept constant for the rest of the experiment. 

Results
First, we were interested in verifying that our group of self-identified aphantasics differed from controls on self-reported levels the vividness of visual mental imagery. VVIQ scores had an average of 73.53 (sd = 43.14) with a range of 32-160. A histogram (Figure 3) of the VVIQ scores indicated that scores were clustered around two means. We tested for bimodality with the multimode package (Ameijeiras-Alonso, Crujeiras, & Rodriguez-Casal, 2021) in R using Hartigans’ dip test (Hartigan & Hartigan, 1985). This test indicated that the distribution of VVIQ scores was multimodal, Dip = 0.096, p < 0.001. The aphantasic group had an average VVIQ score of 43.314 (sd = 21.300) with a range of 32-90. The control group had an average VVIQ score of 119.522 (sd = 21.448) with a range of 96-160. A Welch two-samples t-test indicated that the difference in VVIQ scores between the two groups was significant, t(47) = 13.30, p < .0001, Cohen’s d = 3.57.
We were interested in determining if the performance of aphantasics on a categorization task was differentially impacted by backward masking compared to controls. We modeled accuracy using a linear mixed-effects model in R using the lme4 (Bates, Mächler, Bolker, & Walker, 2015) and lmerTest (using the Satterthwaite method for estimating degrees of freedom for significance testing; Kuznetsova, Brockhoff, & Christensen, 2017) packages, with stimulus duration, backward masking, and aphantasia group as fixed effects variables and participant as a random effect.
For images that were viewed unoccluded (Figure 4), the effect of stimulus duration was not significant ( = .00109, 95% CI [-.00164, .00381], t = 0.78, p = 0.434; Std.  = 0.04, 95% CI [-0.06, 0.13]) and was comparable between the control and aphantasic group ( = -0.43, 95% CI [-0.96, 0.10], t = -1.60, p = 0.110; Std.  = -0.30, 95% CI [-0.65, 0.05]). There was a significant effect of backward masking ( = -0.49, 95% CI [-0.84, -0.14], t = -2.76, p = 0.006; Std.  = -0.09, 95% CI [-0.22, 0.05]), where images backward masked with visual noise disrupted accuracy.
For images that were partially occluded (Figure 5), the model revealed a significant effect of stimulus duration, with accuracy increasing at longer stimulus durations ( = .00172, 95% CI [.00140, .00204], t = 10.54, p < .001; Std.  = 0.45, 95% CI [0.37, 0.54]). A significant effect of backward masking was also observed ( = -0.09, 95% CI [-0.13, -0.04], t = -4.09, p < .001; Std.  = -0.38, 95% CI [-0.50, -0.26]), with accuracy decreasing when images were backward masked with visual noise. There was also a significant effect of aphantasia group, with the aphantasic group having greater accuracy than the control group ( = 0.06, 95% CI [8.28e-04, 0.12], t = 1.99, p = 0.047; Std.  = 0.19, 95% CI [-0.10, 0.49]). However, there was a significant interaction between the effects of backward masking and aphantasia ( = -0.07, 95% CI [-0.13, -7.59e-03], t = -2.21, p = 0.028; Std.  = -0.19, 95% CI [-0.36, -0.01]), such that the aphantasic group was more heavily affected by the visual noise mask. No other interaction effects were significant.
We were also interested in investigating whether any difficulties that aphantasics have in visual processing would be reflected in processing speed (Figure 6). We modeled categorization reaction time with a linear mixed effects model with stimulus duration, backward masking, and aphantasia group as fixed effects variables and participant as a random effect. This model revealed non-significant effects of stimulus duration ( = -.0004, 95% CI [-.0008, .00005], t = -1.74, p = 0.082; Std.  = -0.05, 95% CI [-0.11, 6.36e-03]) and backward masking was not significant ( = -.00152, 95% CI [-0.06, 0.06], t(750) = -0.05, p = 0.959; Std.  = 0.08, 95% CI [-3.51e-03, 0.16]). There was a significant effect of aphantasia group ( = 0.22, 95% CI [0.07, 0.36], t = 2.86, p = 0.004; Std.  = 0.53, 95% CI [0.12, 0.93]), with the aphantasic group having longer reaction times than the control group. No interaction terms were significant.
Because the aphantasic group had higher overall categorization accuracy but also longer reaction times than controls, it is possible that the group difference in performance was a result of a difference in speed-accuracy trade-off. To correct for the influence of speed-accuracy trade-offs, we reran the linear mixed effects model using the Balanced Integration Score (BIS; Liesefeld, Fu, & Zimmer, 2015; Liesefeld & Janczyk, 2019) in place of accuracy. This measure combines speed and accuracy, taking the difference between their standardized scores, and has been found to be less sensitive to speed-accuracy trade-offs relative to other integration measures such as the inverse efficiency score (Townsend & Ashby, 1983), rate-correct score (Woltz & Was, 2006) or the linear-integrated speed-accuracy score (Vandierendonck, 2017). This model (Figure 7) revealed a significant effect of stimulus duration ( = .00888, 95% CI [.00683, 0.01], t = 8.52, p < .001; Std.  = 0.29, 95% CI [0.22, 0.35]), again showing that performance increased with longer stimulus durations. There was also a significant effect of backward masking, ( = -0.38, 95% CI [-0.64, -0.12], t(750) = -2.83, p = 0.005; Std.  = -0.27, 95% CI [-0.36, -0.17]). However, there was no longer a significant main effect of aphantasia group, ( = -0.37, 95% CI [-0.92, 0.18], t(750) = -1.31, p = 0.191; Std.  = -0.29, 95% CI [-0.67, 0.08]), suggesting the main effects in accuracy and reaction time reflected differences in speed-accuracy criterion. Critically, there remained a significant interaction between the effects of backward masking and aphantasia group ( = -0.47, 95% CI [-0.86, -0.08], t(750) = -2.38, p = 0.018; Std.  = -0.10, 95% CI [-0.24, 0.04]), again indicating that the effect of backward masking was greater for the aphantasic group. 
We were also interested in determining if categorization performance could be predicted with VVIQ scores within each group. To that end, we ran separate models for each group that included stimulus duration, backward masking, and VVIQ score as fixed effects and participant as a random effect. However, no main effects or interactions were significant in any of these model fits.

Discussion
Here we asked if the ability of aphantasics to identify the category of an image from a partial cue would be differentially affected by backward masking with visual noise compared to controls.  At the outset we proposed two alternative hypothesis. The first is that aphantasics, due to their hypothesized deficit in to-down reactivation of sensory properties of object represenations (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021), would be more heavily impacted by visual disruption and thus show difficulty in recognizing a partial stimulus when it is presented briefly and followed by a mask. Alternatively, we proposed that aphantasics would be less impacted by visual disruption owing to the tendency of aphantasics to process stimuli at a higher level of abstraction (and therefore not rely on top-down inference of low-level details)(Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Keogh et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023). In support of the first hypothesis, we found that aphantasics were able to successfully categorize objects from the brief presentation of an occluded image, and this ability was significantly reduced, compared to controls, when the cue was followed by the presentation of a visual noise mask. Thus, the latter of the two hypotheses was supported.
The finding that aphantasics were more vulnerable to backward masking compared to controls is revealing, as it suggests that aphantasics may still rely on recurrent processing, but that it is less robust, and therefore more susceptible to backward masking than in control participants. The finding is also consistent with functional neuroimaging, where aphantasics have been shown to have reduced, but not absent, connectivity between frontoparietal executive cortices and visual cortices (Liu, Cohen, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021) and between hippocampus and visual cortices (Monzel et al., 2023). This weaker recurrent connectivity is believed to be the basis for their inability to voluntarily initiate mental imagery (Nadine Dijkstra et al., 2019). Recurrent communication between executive and visual areas is also thought to support perception and pattern completion (N. Dijkstra, Zeidman, Ondobaka, Van Gerven, & Friston, 2017; Tang et al., 2018). Higher order executive regions are thought to identify features from the partial information provided by sensory visual regions. These higher regions then form predictive signals which are sent back to the sensory visual regions which are used to strengthen and prioritize patterns that match these predictions. The predictive signals are thought in turn to create prediction errors, which are then sent back to higher regions (Fyall et al., 2017; Shin et al., 2023). It is this interplay between top-down and bottom-up signals that allows for accurate perception (Den Ouden, Kok, & de Lange, 2012). However, according to this line of thought, it may be that because communication along these pathways is weaker in those with aphantasia, the resulting representations are relatively fragile, and thus more susceptible to visual disruption via backward masking.
Mental imagery, as defined in a review by Pearson and colleagues (Pearson, Naselaris, Holmes, & Kosslyn, 2015), is the representation of sensory information without a direct external stimulus. Under this definition, the retrieval and representation of missing portions of an occluded image via pattern completion can be considered a form of mental imagery, as the represented missing portions are not present in the external stimulus. But given the evidence that the imagery of aphantasics lacks sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023), how is it that aphantasics are able to complete the task? One possibility is that aphantasics have a form of intact unconscious mental imagery that occurs outside their awareness or control. In support of this idea, some aphantasics report having vivid sleeping dreams (Zeman et al., 2020), though use of the term “unconscious” in this manner is debated due to the phenomenological nature of dreams (see Nanay, 2021). Thus, while aphantasics may not be able to voluntarily evoke an image of a beach, top-down visual processes that occur outside of conscious awareness of may still be intact. It should be noted that not all individuals report having dreams, with some indicating they do not experience mental imagery at all, conscious or unconscious. Although, it is be possible that the majority of the aphantasics in the current study had intact unconscious mental imagery, it is not currently clear how to reliably measure this construct in a way that clearly dissociates from conscious imagery.
One thing to note about our results is the lack of within group relationship between VVIQ scores and task performance (categorization of partial images). Given that the VVIQ is thought to measure individual differences in imagery abilities, we would expect that variance in VVIQ scores should correlate with task performance and recurrent processing capability in our control group. Thus, our results raise questions about the construct validity of the VVIQ. Admittedly, our inability to detect a relationship between VVIQ scores and categorization performance may reflect a lack of statistical power; however, concerns over the mixed results on the validity of the VVIQ have been previously raised (Chara & Hamm, 1989) with a recent meta-analysis demonstrating that use of trial-by-trial vividness reports provide higher predictive value than VVIQ scores (Runge, Bakhilau, Omer, & D’Angiulli, 2015). A related issue is that there is little variation in the VVIQ scores of our sample of Aphantasics and samples of other studies. This is partly by design as researchers commonly use a narrow range of scores to diagnose or verify individuals with aphantasia (a range of 16-32 is often used for the VVIQ or 32-64 for the VVIQ2). Despite this, recent surveys suggest that there is a spectrum of imagery experience amongst those with Aphantasia (Zeman et al., 2020).  The limited variation in VVIQ scores among aphantasics may reduce our ability to detect their individual differences in imaging abilities. For example, VVIQ scores predict the degree to which mental imagery affects visual priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) and the pupillary light response (Kay et al., 2022) in control participants. Meanwhile, there is no relationship between VVIQ scores and these behavioral measures in aphantasics. Similarly, we find no relationship between VVIQ scores and categorization performance in our Aphantasic group. Together these results suggest that additional tools or measures are needed to help examine individual differences in imagery in aphantasia.
 A related concern is the heterogeneous nature of aphantasia. With the accumulation of anecdotal and experimental evidence, it is becoming apparent that there exists a wide spectrum of experience with mental imagery among those with aphantasia. Aphantasia does not only describe a complete absence of mental imagery, but also includes those with dim or vague imagery. As mentioned earlier there are individual differences in the ability to dream, and among those who are able to dream, the quality of those dreams varies as well. For some individuals dreams occur infrequently and are visually impoverished compared to controls (Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020; Milton et al., 2021), while others report experiencing highly vivid dreams (Zeman et al., 2020). Recent reports find that individuals with aphantasia tend to be affected in multiple modalities (Dance, Ward, & Simner, 2021; Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020; Alexei Joel Dawes, Keogh, & Pearson, 2023) with 97% of the surveyed sample reporting similar weak imagery in at least one other non-imagery modality, and 62% reporting weak (absent or vague/dim) imagery in all modalities (tactile, gustatory, auditory, bodily, olfactory, movement). While the multimodal nature of aphantasia may not directly speak to the current study, it does suggest a direction of future research on aphantasia as a whole. For example, if recurrent connectivity during mental imagery is generally deficient in aphantasics, the kind of effect we observed in the present study should be reproduced in other modalities. Existing studies have utilized the VVIQ to gauge mental imagery, which primarily measures vividness in the visual domain. Thus, future studies would benefit from expanding scale ranges or developing paradigms that allow for a wider range of imagery experience to be considered, including those in other modalities.
In conclusion, aphantasia describes the condition in which one is unable to voluntarily form vivid mental images. These deficits in imagery are thought to be related to a reduced ability to reinstate perceptual activity in early visual regions via top-down mediation. Supporting this idea, aphantasics have been shown to haveweaker connectivity between frontoparietal executive and visual cortices.  Pattern completion of partial objects is also thought to rely on recurrent connectivity between these regions and this recurrent processing can be probed with the rapid presentation of a backward mask. Here we asked if pattern completion in aphantasics would be differentially impacted by backward masking with visual noise. We had two alternative hypothesis predicting that the ability of aphantasics to categorize partial images would be relatively undisrupted due to their preference for semantic strategies or that they would be more vulnerable to visual disruption due to their weaker recurrent connectivity. Our findings were in line with our second hypothesis, aphantasics were more negatively impacted by visual noise than controls. Our findings suggests that aphantasics have weak, but nevertheless, intact recurrent processing that is more vulnerable to visual interference.

1→About the journal
     2→Aims and scope
     3→A Journal devoted to the Study of the Nervous System and Behavior.
     4→
     5→CORTEX is an international journal devoted to the study of cognition and of the relationship between the nervous system and mental processes, particularly as these are reflected in the behaviour of patients with acquired brain lesions, normal volunteers, children with typical and atypical development, and in the activation of brain regions and systems as recorded by functional neuroimaging techniques. It was founded in 1964 by Ennio De Renzi.
     6→
     7→Article types
     8→Reviews
     9→Present a critical appraisal of the literature on a research issue. No word or reference limit.
    10→
    11→Research reports
    12→These submissions must report important and novel material. No word or reference limit.
    13→
    14→Registered reports  
    15→(click here for more details)
    16→
    17→These submissions undergo a two-phase review process in which study rationale and methodology are considered prior to the research being undertaken. Authors who wish to publish a Registered Report at Cortex should submit via Peer Community in Registered Reports (PCI RR), which performs peer review of Stage 1 and Stage 2 preprints and then offers authors the opportunity to publish their approved manuscript without further peer review in a range of journals (including Cortex). In order to be eligible for publication in Cortex, the Registered Report should satisfy the criteria specified at this link.
    18→
    19→Methods and Assumptions
    20→(click here for more details)
    21→
    22→The Methods and Assumptions section aims to strengthen the methodological foundations of scientific claims in cognitive neuroscience. This section invites empirical or review articles that investigate behavioural or neural testing methods, analysis pipelines or statistical methods, or auxiliary assumptions attached to these. The section also invites tutorials that present and explain best practices to the broader audience of cognitive neuroscientists.
    23→
    24→Exploratory Reports
    25→(click here for more details)
    26→
    27→These studies are inductive/hypothesis-generating, and tend to address relatively open research questions, without strong a priori predictions. Guidelines for their submission can be found here.
    28→
    29→Single case Reports
    30→(click here for more details)
    31→
    32→This section publishes important and novel material using a single case or multiple single cases approach, reporting detailed description of signs and behaviour.
    33→
    34→Discussion Forum
    35→(click here for more details)
    36→
    37→These are organised discussions around a theme of interest for a community of neuropsychologists. The usual format includes a target article, several commentaries, and a rounding off article. Authors wishing to propose a Discussion Forum are invited to peruse those previously published in Cortex, and to contact the Editors.
    38→
    39→Viewpoints
    40→Are mini-review, position papers on relevant topics which do not report new experimental data. They can be submitted spontaneously or invited by editors.
    41→
    42→Commentaries
    43→These submissions may comment, criticize or supplement articles or reports previously published in Cortex. No abstract is required.
    44→
    45→Historical papers
    46→(click here for more details)
    47→
    48→These might be commented translations (or reprinting) of neglected and historical papers or quotations from and/or comments on them.
    49→
    50→Clinical Postcards
    51→(click here for more details)
    52→
    53→Very short insights about some patient or condition, descriptions of symptoms rarely or never reported, interesting observations or incipient theories. The ideas may be tentative rather than fleshed out with experimental data and theoretically clad, but they should be novel.
    54→
    55→Definitions
    56→(click here to view the Guidelines)
    57→
    58→A section within Cortex Discussion Forum dedicated to definitions in neuropsychology. The goal is to try to collect definitions for key neuropsychology terms. A definition should be different from a brief entry in an encyclopaedia or a very brief review. It is the formal statement of the (functional) meaning of a word, an explicit and strict assertion, with no examples or ambiguous terms, allowing the precise identification of the target clinical picture. It must encompass only that information necessary and sufficient to define each term (e.g., anatomical localisation, methods of assessment, etc, should be mentioned only insofar as they are necessary to the definition). See previously published examples here.

    60→Please check the ‘Submitting your manuscript’ section for more detailed instructions.

    62→Peer review
    63→This journal follows a single anonymized review process. Your submission will initially be assessed by our editors to determine suitability for publication in this journal. If your submission is deemed suitable, it will typically be sent to a minimum of two reviewers for an independent expert assessment of the scientific quality. The decision as to whether your article is accepted or rejected will be taken by our editors.

    65→Read more about peer review.

    67→Our editors are not involved in making decisions about papers which:
    68→

    70→

    72→
    73→relate to products or services in which they have an interest.
    74→
    75→Any such submissions will be subject to the journal's usual procedures and peer review will be handled independently of the editor involved and their research group. Read more about editor duties.
    76→
    77→Authors may submit a formal appeal request to the editorial decision, provided the it meets the requirements and follows the procedure outlined in Elsevier’s Appeal Policy. Only one appeal per submission will be considered and the appeal decision will be final.
    78→
    79→Special issues and article collections
    80→The peer review process for special issues and article collections follows the same process as outlined above for regular submissions, except, a guest editor may send the submissions out to the reviewers and may recommend a decision to the journal editor. The journal editor oversees the peer review process of all special issues and article collections to ensure the high standards of publishing ethics and responsiveness are respected and is responsible for the final decision regarding acceptance or rejection of articles.

   193→
   194→Please note: to protect authors’ rights and the confidentiality of their research, this journal does not currently allow the use of generative AI or AI-assisted technologies such as ChatGPT or similar services by reviewers or editors in the peer review and manuscript evaluation process, as is stated in our GenAI Policies for journals. We are actively evaluating compliant AI tools and may revise this policy in the future.
   195→
   196→Preprints
   197→Preprint sharing
   198→Authors may share preprints in line with Elsevier's article sharing policy. Sharing preprints, such as on a preprint server, will not count as prior publication.
   199→
   200→We advise you to read our policy on multiple, redundant or concurrent publication.
   201→
   202→Use of inclusive language
   203→Inclusive language acknowledges diversity, conveys respect to all people, is sensitive to differences, and promotes equal opportunities. Authors should ensure their work uses inclusive language throughout and contains nothing which might imply one individual is superior to another on the grounds of:
   204→
   205→age
   206→
   207→gender
   208→
   209→race
   210→
   211→ethnicity
   212→
   213→culture
   214→
   215→sexual orientation
   216→
   217→disability or health condition
   218→
   219→We recommend avoiding the use of descriptors about personal attributes unless they are relevant and valid. Write for gender neutrality with the use of plural nouns ("clinicians, patients/clients") as default. Wherever possible, avoid using "he, she," or "he/she."
   220→
   221→No assumptions should be made about the beliefs of readers and writing should be free from bias, stereotypes, slang, reference to dominant culture and/or cultural assumptions.
   222→
   223→These guidelines are meant as a point of reference to help you identify appropriate language but are by no means exhaustive or definitive.
   224→
   225→Reporting sex- and gender-based analyses
   226→There is no single, universally agreed-upon set of guidelines for defining sex and gender. We offer the following guidance:
   227→
   228→Sex and gender-based analyses (SGBA) should be integrated into research design when research involves or pertains to humans, animals or eukaryotic cells. This should be done in accordance with any requirements set by funders or sponsors and best practices within a field.
   229→
   230→Sex and/or gender dimensions of the research should be addressed within the article or declared as a limitation to the generalizability of the research.
   231→
   232→Definitions of sex and/or gender applied should be explicitly stated to enhance the precision, rigor and reproducibility of the research and to avoid ambiguity or conflation of terms and the constructs to which they refer.
   233→
   234→We advise you to read the Sex and Gender Equity in Research (SAGER) guidelines and the SAGER checklist (PDF) on the EASE website, which offer systematic approaches to the use of sex and gender information in study design, data analysis, outcome reporting and research interpretation.
   235→
   236→For further information we suggest reading the rationale behind and recommended use of the SAGER guidelines.
   237→
   238→Definitions of sex and/or gender
   239→
   240→We ask authors to define how sex and gender have been used in their research and publication. Some guidance:
   241→
   242→Sex generally refers to a set of biological attributes that are associated with physical and physiological features such as chromosomal genotype, hormonal levels, internal and external anatomy. A binary sex categorization (male/female) is usually designated at birth ("sex assigned at birth") and is in most cases based solely on the visible external anatomy of a newborn. In reality, sex categorizations include people who are intersex/have differences of sex development (DSD).
   243→
   244→Gender generally refers to socially constructed roles, behaviors and identities of women, men and gender-diverse people that occur in a historical and cultural context and may vary across societies and over time. Gender influences how people view themselves and each other, how they behave and interact and how power is distributed in society.
   245→
   246→Jurisdictional claims
   247→Elsevier respects the decisions taken by its authors as to how they choose to designate territories and identify their affiliations in their published content. Elsevier’s policy is to take a neutral position with respect to territorial disputes or jurisdictional claims, including, but not limited to, maps and institutional affiliations. For journals that Elsevier publishes on behalf of a third party owner, the owner may set its own policy on these issues.
   248→
   249→Maps: Readers should be able to locate any study areas shown within maps using common mapping platforms. Maps should only show the area actually studied and authors should not include a location map which displays a larger area than the bounding box of the study area. Authors should add a note clearly stating that "map lines delineate study areas and do not necessarily depict accepted national boundaries”.  During the review process, Elsevier’s editors may request authors to change maps if these guidelines are not followed.
   250→
   251→Institutional affiliations: Authors should use either the full, standard title of their institution or the standard abbreviation of the institutional name so that the institutional name can be independently verified for research integrity purposes.
   252→
   253→Studies in humans
   254→Authors must follow ethical guidelines for studies carried out in humans.
   255→
   256→Work which involves the use of human subjects should be carried out in accordance with the World Medical Association Declaration of Helsinki: Ethical principles for medical research involving human subjects.
   257→
   258→Manuscripts should follow the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE) recommendations for the conduct, reporting, editing and publication of scholarly work in medical journals and aim to be representative of human populations in terms of sex, age and ethnicity. Sex and gender terms should be used correctly, as outlined by WHO (World Health Organization).
   259→
   260→Manuscripts must include a statement that all procedures were performed in compliance with relevant laws and institutional guidelines and have been approved by the appropriate institutional committee(s). The statement should contain the date and reference number of the ethical approval(s) obtained.
   261→
   262→Manuscripts must also include a statement that the privacy rights of human subjects have been observed and that informed consent was obtained for experimentation with human subjects.
   263→
   264→This journal will not accept manuscripts that contain data derived from unethically sourced organs or tissue, including from executed prisoners or prisoners of conscience, consistent with recommendations by Global Rights Compliance on Mitigating Human Rights Risks in Transplantation Medicine. For all studies that use human organs or tissues, sufficient evidence must be provided that these were procured in line with WHO Guiding Principles on Human Cell, Tissue and Organ Transplantation. For clinical studies, a statement of informed consent having been obtained from a patient or their nominated representative, paired with ethical approval for the study from a suitable institution, as required by the policies of the journal, may be considered sufficient evidence, but the journal reserves the right to request additional evidence in cases where it feels this is not sufficient. The source of the organs or tissues used in clinical research must be transparent and traceable. If your manuscript describes organ transplantation you must additionally declare within the manuscript that:
   265→
   266→autonomous consent free from coercion was obtained from the donor(s) or their next of kin.
   267→
   268→organs and/or tissues were not sourced from executed prisoners or prisoners of conscience.
   269→
   270→Informed consent and patient details
   271→Authors must document in the manuscript that ethics committee approval and informed consent have been obtained for studies involving patients or volunteers (including organ/tissue donors). Key guidelines:
   272→
   273→Appropriate consents, permissions and releases must be obtained if case details, personal information and images of patients or any other individuals are included in a publication, even if anonymized.
   274→
   275→Patient and research subjects’ names, initials, hospital or social security numbers, dates of birth or any other personal or identifying information should never be used, even where consent has been provided.
   276→
   277→Written consents must be retained. They should not be provided to this journal unless this is specifically requested in exceptional circumstances, for example, when a legal issue arises. Only then should you provide copies of the consents, or evidence that all relevant consents were obtained.
   278→
   279→Personal details of any patient must only be included in your article or in any supplementary materials (including all images and videos) in cases where written permission has been given by the patient (or, where applicable, the next of kin).
   280→
   281→We advise you to review Elsevier’s policy on patient consent prior to preparing your manuscript.
   282→
   283→Writing and formatting
   284→File format
   285→We ask you to provide editable source files for your entire submission (including figures, tables and text graphics). Some guidelines:
   286→
   287→Save files in an editable format, using the extension .doc/.docx for Word files and .tex for LaTeX files. A PDF is not an acceptable source file.
   288→
   289→Lay out text in a single-column format.
   290→
   291→Remove any strikethrough and underlined text from your manuscript, unless it has scientific significance related to your article.
   292→
   293→Use spell-check and grammar-check functions to avoid errors.
   294→
   295→We advise you to read our Step-by-step guide to publishing with Elsevier.
   296→
   297→Title page
   298→You are required to include the following details in the title page information:
   299→
   300→Article title. Article titles should be concise and informative. Please avoid abbreviations and formulae, where possible, unless they are established and widely understood, e.g., DNA).
   301→
   302→Author names. Provide the given name(s) and family name(s) of each author. The order of authors should match the order in the submission system. Carefully check that all names are accurately spelled. If needed, you can add your name between parentheses in your own script after the English transliteration.
   303→
   304→Affiliations. Add affiliation addresses, referring to where the work was carried out, below the author names. Indicate affiliations using a lower-case superscript letter immediately after the author's name and in front of the corresponding address. Ensure that you provide the full postal address of each affiliation, including the country name and, if available, the email address of each author.
   305→
   306→Corresponding author. Clearly indicate who will handle correspondence for your article at all stages of the refereeing and publication process and also post-publication. This responsibility includes answering any future queries about your results, data, methodology and materials. It is important that the email address and contact details of your corresponding author are kept up to date during the submission and publication process.
   307→
   308→Present/permanent address. If an author has moved since the work described in your article was carried out, or the author was visiting during that time, a "present address" (or "permanent address") can be indicated by a footnote to the author's name. The address where the author carried out the work must be retained as their main affiliation address. Use superscript Arabic numerals for such footnotes.
   309→
   310→Abstract
   311→You are required to provide a concise and factual abstract which does not exceed 250 words. The abstract should briefly state the purpose of your research, principal results and major conclusions. Some guidelines:
   312→
   313→Abstracts must be able to stand alone as abstracts are often presented separately from the article.
   314→
   315→Avoid references. If any are essential to include, ensure that you cite the author(s) and year(s).
   316→
   317→Avoid non-standard or uncommon abbreviations. If any are essential to include, ensure they are defined within your abstract at first mention.
   318→
   319→Keywords
   320→You are required to provide 1 to 7 keywords for indexing purposes. Keywords should be written in English. Please try to avoid keywords consisting of multiple words (using "and" or "of").
   321→
   322→We recommend that you only use abbreviations in keywords if they are firmly established in the field.
   323→
   324→Highlights
   325→You are encouraged to provide article highlights at submission.
   326→
   327→Highlights are a short collection of bullet points that should capture the novel results of your research as well as any new methods used during your study. Highlights will help increase the discoverability of your article via search engines. Some guidelines:
   328→
   329→Submit highlights as a separate editable file in the online submission system with the word "highlights" included in the file name.
   330→
   331→Highlights should consist of 3 to 5 bullet points, each a maximum of 85 characters, including spaces.
   332→
   333→We encourage you to view example article highlights and read about the benefits of their inclusion.
   334→
   335→Graphical abstract
   336→You are encouraged to provide a graphical abstract at submission.
   337→
   338→The graphical abstract should summarize the contents of your article in a concise, pictorial form which is designed to capture the attention of a wide readership. A graphical abstract will help draw more attention to your online article and support readers in digesting your research. Some guidelines:
   339→
   340→Submit your graphical abstract as a separate file in the online submission system.
   341→
   342→Ensure the image is a minimum of 531 x 1328 pixels (h x w) or proportionally more and is readable at a size of 5 x 13 cm using a regular screen resolution of 96 dpi.
   343→
   344→Our preferred file types for graphical abstracts are TIFF, EPS, PDF or MS Office files.
   345→
   346→We encourage you to view example graphical abstracts and read about the benefits of including them.
   347→
   348→Units, classifications codes and nomenclature
   349→This journal requires you to use the international system of units (SI) which follows internationally accepted rules and conventions. If other units are mentioned within your article, you should provide the equivalent unit in SI.
   350→
   351→Math formulae
   352→Submit math equations as editable text, not as images.
   353→
   354→Present simple formulae in line with normal text, where possible.
   355→
   356→Use the solidus (/) instead of a horizontal line for small fractional terms such as X/Y.
   357→
   358→Present variables in italics.
   359→
   360→Denote powers of e by exp.
   361→
   362→Display equations separately from your text, numbering them consecutively in the order they are referred to within your text.
   363→
   364→Tables
   365→Tables must be submitted as editable text, not as images. Some guidelines:
   366→
   367→Place tables next to the relevant text or on a separate page(s) at the end of your article.
   368→
   369→Cite all tables in the manuscript text.
   370→
   371→Number tables consecutively according to their appearance in the text.
   372→
   373→Please provide captions along with the tables.
   374→
   375→Place any table notes below the table body.
   376→
   377→Avoid vertical rules and shading within table cells.
   378→
   379→We recommend that you use tables sparingly, ensuring that any data presented in tables is not duplicating results described elsewhere in the article.
   380→
   381→Figures, images and artwork
   382→Figures, images, artwork, diagrams and other graphical media must be supplied as separate files along with the manuscript. We recommend that you read our detailed artwork and media instructions. Some excerpts:
   383→
   384→When submitting artwork:
   385→
   386→Cite all images in the manuscript text.
   387→
   388→Number images according to the sequence they appear within your article.
   389→
   390→Submit each image as a separate file using a logical naming convention for your files (for example, Figure_1, Figure_2 etc).
   391→
   392→Please provide captions for all figures, images, and artwork.
   393→
   394→Text graphics may be embedded in the text at the appropriate position. If you are working with LaTeX, text graphics may also be embedded in the file.
   395→
   396→Artwork formats
   397→When your artwork is finalized, "save as" or convert your electronic artwork to the formats listed below taking into account the given resolution requirements for line drawings, halftones, and line/halftone combinations:
   398→
   399→Vector drawings: Save as EPS or PDF files embedding the font or saving the text as "graphics."
   400→
   401→Color or grayscale photographs (halftones): Save as TIFF, JPG or PNG files using a minimum of 300 dpi (for single column: min. 1063 pixels, full page width: 2244 pixels).
   402→
   403→Bitmapped line drawings: Save as TIFF, JPG or PNG files using a minimum of 1000 dpi (for single column: min. 3543 pixels, full page width: 7480 pixels).
   404→
   405→Combinations bitmapped line/halftones (color or grayscale): Save as TIFF, JPG or PNG files using a minimum of 500 dpi (for single column: min. 1772 pixels, full page width: 3740 pixels).
   406→
   407→Please do not submit:
   408→
   409→files that are too low in resolution (for example, files optimized for screen use such as GIF, BMP, PICT or WPG files).
   410→
   411→disproportionally large images compared to font size, as text may become unreadable.
   412→
   413→Figure captions
   414→All images must have a caption. A caption should consist of a brief title (not displayed on the figure itself) and a description of the image. We advise you to keep the amount of text in any image to a minimum, though any symbols and abbreviations used should be explained.
   415→
   416→Provide captions in a separate file.
   417→
   418→Color artwork
   419→If you submit usable color figures with your accepted article, we will ensure that they appear in color online.
   420→
   421→Please ensure that color images are accessible to all, including those with impaired color vision. Learn more about color and web accessibility.
   422→
   423→Generative AI and Figures, images and artwork
   424→Please read our policy on the use of generative AI and AI-assisted tools in figures, images and artwork, which can be found in Elsevier’s GenAI Policies for Journals. This policy states:
   425→
   426→We do not permit the use of Generative AI or AI-assisted tools to create or alter images in submitted manuscripts.
   427→
   428→The only exception is if the use of AI or AI-assisted tools is part of the research design or methods (for example, in the field of biomedical imaging). If this is the case, such use must be described in a reproducible manner in the methods section, including the name of the model or tool, version and extension numbers, and manufacturer.
   429→
   430→The use of generative AI or AI-assisted tools in the production of artwork such as for graphical abstracts is not permitted. The use of generative AI in the production of cover art may in some cases be allowed, if the author obtains prior permission from the journal editor and publisher, can demonstrate that all necessary rights have been cleared for the use of the relevant material, and ensures that there is correct content attribution.
   431→
   432→Supplementary material
   433→We encourage the use of supplementary materials such as applications, images and sound clips to enhance research. Some guidelines:
   434→
   435→Supplementary material should be accurate and relevant to the research.
   436→
   437→Cite all supplementary files in the manuscript text.
   438→
   439→Submit supplementary materials at the same time as your article. Be aware that all supplementary materials provided will appear online in the exact same file type as received. These files will not be formatted or typeset by the production team.
   440→
   441→Include a concise, descriptive caption for each supplementary file describing its content.
   442→
   443→Provide updated files if at any stage of the publication process you wish to make changes to submitted supplementary materials.
   444→
   445→Do not make annotations or corrections to a previous version of a supplementary file.
   446→
   447→Switch off the option to track changes in Microsoft Office files. If tracked changes are left on, they will appear in your published version.
   448→
   449→Video
   450→This journal accepts video material and animation sequences to support and enhance your scientific research. We encourage you to include links to video or animation files within articles. Some guidelines:
   451→
   452→When including video or animation file links within your article, refer to the video or animation content by adding a note in your text where the file should be placed.
   453→
   454→Clearly label files ensuring the given file name is directly related to the file content.
   455→
   456→Provide files in one of our recommended file formats. Files should be within our preferred maximum file size of 150 MB per file, 1 GB in total.
   457→
   458→Provide "stills" for each of your files. These will be used as standard icons to personalize the link to your video data. You can choose any frame from your video or animation or make a separate image.
   459→
   460→Provide text (for both the electronic and the print version) to be placed in the portions of your article that refer to the video content. This is essential text, as video and animation files cannot be embedded in the print version of the journal.
   461→
   462→We publish all video and animation files supplied in the electronic version of your article.
   463→
   464→For more detailed instructions, we recommend that you read our guidelines on submitting video content to be included in the body of an article.
   465→
   466→Research data
   467→We are committed to supporting the storage of, access to and discovery of research data, and our research data policy sets out the principles guiding how we work with the research community to support a more efficient and transparent research process.
   468→
   469→Research data refers to the results of observations or experimentation that validate research findings, which may also include software, code, models, algorithms, protocols, methods and other useful materials related to the project.
   470→
   471→Please read our guidelines on sharing research data for more information on depositing, sharing and using research data and other relevant research materials.
   472→
   473→For this journal, the following instructions from our research data guidelines apply.
   474→
   475→Option C: Research data deposit, citation and linking
   476→
   477→You are required to:
   478→
   479→Deposit your research data in a relevant data repository.
   480→
   481→Cite and link to this dataset in your article.
   482→
   483→If this is not possible, make a statement explaining why research data cannot be shared.
   484→
   485→Data statement
   486→To foster transparency, you are required to state the availability of any data at submission.
   487→
   488→Ensuring data is available may be a requirement of your funding body or institution. If your data is unavailable to access or unsuitable to post, you can state the reason why (e.g., your research data includes sensitive or confidential information such as patient data) during the submission process. This statement will appear with your published article on ScienceDirect.
   489→
   490→Read more about the importance and benefits of providing a data statement.
   491→
   492→Data linking
   493→Linking to the data underlying your work increases your exposure and may lead to new collaborations. It also provides readers with a better understanding of the described research.
   494→
   495→If your research data has been made available in a data repository there are a number of ways your article can be linked directly to the dataset:
   496→
   497→Provide a link to your dataset when prompted during the online submission process.
   498→
   499→For some data repositories, a repository banner will automatically appear next to your published article on ScienceDirect.
   500→
   501→You can also link relevant data or entities within the text of your article through the use of identifiers. Use the following format: Database: 12345 (e.g. TAIR: AT1G01020; CCDC: 734053; PDB: 1XFN).
   502→
   503→Learn more about linking research data and research articles in ScienceDirect.
   504→
   505→Research Elements
   506→This journal enables the publication of research objects (e.g. data, methods, protocols, software and hardware) related to original research in Elsevier's Research Elements journals.
   507→
   508→Research Elements are peer-reviewed, open access journals which make research objects findable, accessible and reusable. By providing detailed descriptions of objects and their application with links to the original research article, your research objects can be placed into context within your article.
   509→
   510→You will be alerted during submission to the opportunity to submit a manuscript to one of the Research Elements journals. Your Research Elements article can be prepared by you, or by one of your collaborators.
   511→
   512→Article structure
   513→Article sections
   514→Divide your article into clearly defined and numbered sections. Number subsections 1.1 (then 1.1.1, 1.1.2, ...), then 1.2, etc.
   515→
   516→Use the numbering format when cross-referencing within your article. Do not just refer to "the text."
   517→
   518→You may give subsections a brief heading. Headings should appear on a separate line.
   519→
   520→Do not include the article abstract within section numbering.
   521→
   522→Theory and calculation
   523→The theory section should lay the foundation for further work by extending the background you provided in the introduction to your article. The calculation section should represent a practical development from a theoretical basis.
   524→
   525→Glossary
   526→Please provide definitions of field-specific terms used in your article, in a separate list.
   527→
   528→Acknowledgements
   529→Include any individuals who provided you with help during your research, such as help with language, writing or proof reading, in the acknowledgements section. Acknowledgements should be placed in a separate section which appears directly before the reference list. Do not include acknowledgements on your title page, as a footnote to your title, or anywhere else in your article other than in the separate acknowledgements section.
   530→
   531→Author contributions: CRediT
   532→Corresponding authors are required to acknowledge co-author contributions using CRediT (Contributor Roles Taxonomy) roles:
   533→
   534→Conceptualization
   535→
   536→Data curation
   537→
   538→Formal analysis
   539→
   540→Funding acquisition
   541→
   542→Investigation
   543→
   544→Methodology
   545→
   546→Project administration
   547→
   548→Resources
   549→
   550→Software
   551→
   552→Supervision
   553→
   554→Validation
   555→
   556→Visualization
   557→
   558→Writing – original draft
   559→
   560→Writing – review and editing
   561→
   562→Not all CRediT roles will apply to every manuscript and some authors may contribute through multiple roles.
   563→
   564→We advise you to read more about CRediT and view an example of a CRediT author statement.
   565→
   566→Funding sources
   567→Authors must disclose any funding sources who provided financial support for the conduct of the research and/or preparation of the article. The role of sponsors, if any, should be declared in relation to the study design, collection, analysis and interpretation of data, writing of the report and decision to submit the article for publication. If funding sources had no such involvement this should be stated in your submission.
   568→
   569→List funding sources in this standard way to facilitate compliance to funder's requirements:
   570→
   571→Funding: This work was supported by the National Institutes of Health [grant numbers xxxx, yyyy]; the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, Seattle, WA [grant number zzzz]; and the United States Institutes of Peace [grant number aaaa].
   572→
   573→It is not necessary to include detailed descriptions on the program or type of grants, scholarships and awards. When funding is from a block grant or other resources available to a university, college, or other research institution, submit the name of the institute or organization that provided the funding.
   574→
   575→If no funding has been provided for the research, it is recommended to include the following sentence:
   576→
   577→This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
   578→
   579→Appendices
   580→We ask you to use the following format for appendices:
   581→
   582→Identify individual appendices within your article using the format: A, B, etc.
   583→
   584→Give separate numbering to formulae and equations within appendices using formats such as Eq. (A.1), Eq. (A.2), etc. and in subsequent appendices, Eq. (B.1), Eq. (B. 2) etc. In a similar way, give separate numbering to tables and figures using formats such as Table A.1; Fig. A.1, etc.
   585→
   586→References
   587→References within text
   588→Any references cited within your article should also be present in your reference list and vice versa. Some guidelines:
   589→
   590→References cited in your abstract must be given in full.
   591→
   592→We recommend that you do not include unpublished results and personal communications in your reference list, though you may mention them in the text of your article.
   593→
   594→Any unpublished results and personal communications included in your reference list must follow the standard reference style of the journal. In substitution of the publication date add "unpublished results" or "personal communication."
   595→
   596→References cited as "in press" imply that the item has been accepted for publication.
   597→
   598→Linking to cited sources will increase the discoverability of your research.
   599→
   600→Before submission, check that all data provided in your reference list are correct, including any references which have been copied. Providing correct reference data allows us to link to abstracting and indexing services such as Scopus, Crossref and PubMed. Any incorrect surnames, journal or book titles, publication years or pagination within your references may prevent link creation.
   601→
   602→We encourage the use of Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs) as reference links as they provide a permanent link to the electronic article referenced.
   603→
   604→Reference format
   605→This journal does not set strict requirements on reference formatting at submission. Some guidelines:
   606→
   607→References can be in any style or format as long as the style is consistent.
   608→
   609→Author names, journal or book titles, chapter or article titles, year of publication, volume numbers, article numbers or pagination must be included, where applicable.
   610→
   611→Use of DOIs is recommended.
   612→
   613→Our journal reference style will be applied to your article after acceptance, at proof stage. If required, at this stage we will ask you to correct or supply any missing reference data.
   614→
   615→Reference style
   616→Citations in the text should follow the referencing style used by the American Psychological Association. You are referred to the Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association, Seventh Edition (2020) ISBN 978-1-4338-3215-4.
   617→
   618→The reference list should be arranged alphabetically and then chronologically. More than one reference from the same author(s) in the same year must be identified by the letters 'a', 'b', 'c', etc., placed after the year of publication.
   619→
   620→Examples:
   621→
   622→Reference to a journal publication:
   623→
   624→Van der Geer, J., Handgraaf T., & Lupton, R. A. (2020). The art of writing a scientific article. Journal of Scientific Communications, 163, 51–59. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sc.2020.00372.
   625→
   626→Reference to a journal publication with an article number:  
   627→
   628→Van der Geer, J., Handgraaf, T., & Lupton, R. A. (2022). The art of writing a scientific article. Heliyon, 19, Article e00205. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e00205.
   629→
   630→Reference to a book:
   631→
   632→Strunk, W., Jr., & White, E. B. (2000). The elements of style (4th ed.). Longman (Chapter 4).
   633→
   634→Reference to a chapter in a book:
   635→
   636→Mettam, G. R., & Adams, L. B. (2020). How to prepare an electronic version of your article. In B. S. Jones, & R. Z. Smith (Eds.), Introduction to the electronic age (pp. 281–304). E-Publishing Inc.
   637→
   638→Reference to a website:
   639→
   640→Powertech Systems. (2022). Lithium-ion vs lead-acid cost analysis. Retrieved from http://www.powertechsystems.eu/home/tech-corner/lithium-ion-vs-lead-acid-cost-analysis/. Accessed January 6, 2022.
   641→
   642→Reference to a dataset:
   643→
   644→Oguro, M., Imahiro, S., Saito, S., & Nakashizuka, T. (2015). Mortality data for Japanese oak wilt disease and surrounding forest compositions [dataset]. Mendeley Data, v1. https://doi.org/10.17632/xwj98nb39r.1.
   645→
   646→Reference to a conference paper or poster presentation:
   647→
   648→Engle, E.K., Cash, T.F., & Jarry, J.L. (2019, November). The Body Image Behaviours Inventory-3: Development and validation of the Body Image Compulsive Actions and Body Image Avoidance Scales. Poster session presentation at the meeting of the Association for Behavioural and Cognitive Therapies, New York, NY.
   649→
   650→Reference to software:
   651→
   652→Coon, E., Berndt, M., Jan, A., Svyatsky, D., Atchley, A., Kikinzon, E., Harp, D., Manzini, G., Shelef, E., Lipnikov, K., Garimella, R., Xu, C., Moulton, D., Karra, S., Painter, S., Jafarov, E., & Molins, S. (2020). Advanced Terrestrial Simulator (ATS) (Version 0.88) [Computer software]. Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3727209.
   653→
   654→Web references
   655→When listing web references, as a minimum you should provide the full URL and the date when the reference was last accessed. Additional information (e.g. DOI, author names, dates or reference to a source publication) should also be provided, if known.
   656→
   657→You can list web references separately under a new heading directly after your reference list or include them in your reference list.
   658→
   659→Data references
   660→We encourage you to cite underlying or relevant datasets within article text and to list data references in the reference list.
   661→
   662→When citing data references, you should include:
   663→
   664→author name(s)
   665→
   666→dataset title
   667→
   668→data repository
   669→
   670→version (where available)
   671→
   672→year
   673→
   674→global persistent identifier
   675→
   676→Add [dataset] immediately before your reference. This will help us to properly identify the dataset. The [dataset] identifier will not appear in your published article.
   677→
   678→Preprint references
   679→We ask you to mark preprints clearly. You should include the word "preprint" or the name of the preprint server as part of your reference and provide the preprint DOI.
   680→
   681→Where a preprint has subsequently become available as a peer-reviewed publication, use the formal publication as your reference.
   682→
   683→If there are preprints that are central to your work or that cover crucial developments in the topic, but they are not yet formally published, you may reference the preprint.
   684→
   685→Reference management software
   686→Most Elsevier journals have their reference template available in popular reference management software products. These include products that support Citation Style Language (CSL) such as Mendeley Reference Manager.
   687→
   688→If you use a citation plug-in from these products, select the relevant journal template and all your citations and bibliographies will automatically be formatted in the journal style. We advise you to remove all field codes before submitting your manuscript to any reference management software product.
   689→
   690→If a template is not available for this journal, follow the format given in examples in the reference style section of this Guide for Authors.
   691→
   692→Submitting your manuscript
   693→Submission checklist
   694→Before completing the submission of your manuscript, we advise you to read our submission checklist:
   695→
   696→One author has been designated as the corresponding author and their full contact details (email address, full postal address and phone numbers) have been provided.
   697→
   698→All files have been uploaded, including keywords, figure captions and tables (including a title, description and footnotes) included.
   699→
   700→Spelling and grammar checks have been carried out.
   701→
   702→All references in the article text are cited in the reference list and vice versa.
   703→
   704→Permission has been obtained for the use of any copyrighted material from other sources, including the Web.
   705→
   706→For open access articles, all authors understand that they are responsible for payment of the article publishing charge (APC) if the manuscript is accepted. Payment of the APC may be covered by the corresponding author's institution, or the research funder.
   707→
   708→Journal specific information
   709→All empirical articles (those reporting research data) published in Cortex must satisfy our standards of transparency and openness, which require authors to share—to the maximum extent possible—the data, code and materials used in the research, and to provide further details about the study design and any preregistration. The requirements are specified in our Transparency and Openness Promotion guidelines.
   710→
   711→To verify compliance with these standards, all empirical articles must be accompanied by a Scientific Transparency Statement (STS) and a Scientific Transparency Report (STR), which specify the sharing and public access routes for data, code and materials, and further details about study design and preregistration. These documents are not required for the initial submission, but are mandatory uploads when you are invited to submit a revised manuscript for an empirical article. CORTEX transparency editors will check these documents to ensure that your manuscript complies with the requirements. The manuscript cannot be published until the requirements are met.
   712→
   713→The easiest and best way to create the STS and STR is to use the Cortex transparency app, at the following address: https://cortex-top.shinyapps.io/top-report/. Alternatively, you can create the documents from a standard template. For further guidance and templates, see: CORTEX Scientific Transparency Statement and Scientific Transparency Report, guidance
   714→
   715→After receiving a final decision
   716→Article Transfer Service
