content.content:
7→Literature Review
     8→When asked to picture a summer’s day on the beach, with cloudless blue skies and crashing waves, many people claim to be able to “see” an image in their “mind’s eye”. This ability to visualize experiences in the mind’s eye is referred to as mental imagery. Mental imagery is thought to be related to a number of cognitive activities, including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).
     9→However, whether or not mental imagery has functional importance for cognition has been the subject of a long-standing debate (see Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). One way to gain insight into the relationship between mental imagery and cognitive processes is to investigate how individual differences in mental imagery abilities relate to more direct indices of cognitive performance. To gauge imagery strength, researchers have taken to collecting trial-by-trial self-reports of vividness or alternatively have used tools such as the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023) to quantify trait differences in mental imagery. Trial-to-trial variation in self-reported vividness ratings have been found to correlate with the number of visual details reported in autobiographical memories (D’Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), the degree of neural reinstatement during memory recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015) and the degree of neural overlap between imagery and perception (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). In addition, VVIQ scores predict the degree to which an mental imagery induces priming during binocular rivalry tasks (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011); and the size of pupillary light response when imagining bright versus dim stimuli (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). Thus, there is ample evidence suggesting that self-reported measures of mental imagery strength relates to neural and behavioral measures of cognitive performance in memory and perception tasks.
    10→Interestingly, these self-reports have revealed individuals who have little to no experience with mental images. Cases of a lack of imagery have been reported since the 19th century (Galton, 1880), a condition now referred to as Aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015). Aphantasia is relatively rare, with recent reports indicating a prevalence rate as low as 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to as high as 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia (henceforth referred to as aphantasics) often interpret the phrase “seeing something in your mind” as a metaphor, and are surprised to learn that others actually report experiencing something more literally image-like (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). On the surface, aphantasics lead normal lives, notwithstanding the evidence reviewed above suggesting that mental imagery plays a role in a number of cognitive activities. How might their lack of mental imagery affect their cognition? 
    11→When interviewed about their autobiographical memory, aphantasics report fewer episodic and visual details, with this effect being more prominent when they are asked to simulate hypothetical future events (Alexei J. Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Alexei J. Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020) suggesting a connection to a relative phemonon referred to as “severely deficient autobiographical memory” (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018).  Aphantasics do, however, report having dreams, suggesting that they may retain unconscious forms of mental imagery (Zeman et al., 2020), although it has been reported that aphantasics dream less frequently than controls and that these dreams tend to be less vivid (Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020). While the occurrence of unconscious mental imagery might suggest that aphantasia results from a failure of metacognition, behavioural studies have shown that they neither exhibit imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018), nor a change in the pupillary light response when imagining stimuli of different brightness (Kay et al., 2022). These findings have been taken to suggest that their mental representations indeed lack sensory and phenomenal properties. Taken together, the above-reviewed research suggests that aphantasics tend to process visual information at higher levels of abstraction. Indeed, when performing visual working memory tasks, aphantasics do as well as controls, but report having used strategies  that make use of semantic labels (Keogh et al., 2021) rather than lower level sensory information.
    12→What neurological mechanisms might underlie aphantasia? Neuroimaging studies have demonstrated that the contents of their imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) can be decoded in the early visual cortex, suggesting content specific information is present in early visual cortex representations of aphantasics. However, the content of imagery could not be cross-decoded with activity from perception (Meng et al., 2023) suggesting that the information that aphantasics store in these representations appears to be different than their representations formed during perception. Additionally when aphantasics were asked to imagine a stimulus at a specific location, neural activity was greater in the ipsilateral hemisphere whereas in controls activity was greater in the contralateral hemisphere (Meng et al., 2023). Together these findings suggest that the imagery related representations of aphantasics either contains less—or transformed—sensory information compared to controls.
    13→Another functional neuroimaging report showed that aphantasics exhibit reduced resting state connectivity between prefrontal cortices and visual cortices and less left anterior parietal activity when asked to actively visualize faces and scenes (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). Because mental imagery is believed to be supported by the top-down activation of sensory areas, it is thought to rely more heavily on recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices compared to perception (Nadine Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). Thus, the reduced connectivity might partly underlie the aphantasic inability to  voluntarily create mental images.
    14→Similar findings were observed in an fMRI study examining the autobiographical memory deficits of aphantasics (Monzel et al., 2023). When asked to retrieve an autobiographical memory, aphantasics were shown to have reduced activation in bilateral hippocampus, increased activation of the visual cortices, and reduced connectivity between hippocampus and visual cortices. This increased activation of visual cortices during memory retrieval was particularly interesting, and suggested that the deficits to autobiographical memory may be related to a reduced signal-to-noise ratio caused by the hyper activity of visual cortices (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023).
    15→In this paper, we ask how perceptual pattern completion, another process theorized to depend on recurrent connections (Rolls, 2013), might be impacted in aphantasia. Perceptual pattern completion enables us to recognize a partially degraded or occluded object. Activity from the partial object is thought to spread to the missing features through both lateral and vertical recurrent connections (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), and this has been supported with evidence from computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). This recurrent processing can be inhibited by backward masking stimuli with visual noise, impairing recognition (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O’Reilly, 2012). When a partial stimulus is viewed, higher areas process the resulting feedforward signals, forming predictions about the identity of the stimulus and send top-down feedback signals to lower areas, strengthening and reinforcing neural responses in lower areas. However, when a mask quickly follows the stimulus as in backward masking, feedforward signals from the mask travel up the processing stream at the same time as the feedback prediction signals related to the previous stimulus. This mismatch between feedforward and feedback signals is thought to be the cause of the observed impairments to perception by backward masking (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).
    16→How might pattern completion in aphantasics be affected by visual disruption via backward masking? Backward masking is believed to selectively disrupt recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000), and because aphantasics have reduced connectivity between frontoparietal and visual cortices (Milton et al., 2021), they might be more heavily impacted by disruption and thus show difficulty in recognizing a partial stimulus when it is presented briefly and followed by a mask. However, aphantasics tend to favour semantic strategies when completing visual tasks (Keogh et al., 2021) and their imagery related presentations appear to have less sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023). It therefore may be the case that aphantasics are able to perform a kind of high-level pattern completion by relying on the retrieval of semantic details when presented with a partial visual stimulus. Thus, an alternative hypothesis is that, due to aphantasics’ tendency to process stimuli at a higher level of abstraction (and therefore not rely on top-down inference of low-level details), backward masking might have a reduced impact on their ability to recognize the partial stimulus. In the present study, we test these alternative hypotheses, in a group of self-identified aphantasics and matched controls that completed a task where we briefly presented partially occluded images depicting objects and asked participants to identify the object as belonging to one of four categories. Crucially, these partially occluded images were sometimes followed by a visual noise mask, aimed at disrupting recurrent processing that is thought to support pattern completion.

Literature Review
When asked to picture a summer’s day on the beach, with cloudless blue skies and crashing waves, many people claim to be able to “see” an image in their “mind’s eye”. This ability to visualize experiences in the mind’s eye is referred to as mental imagery. Mental imagery is thought to be related to a number of cognitive activities, including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).
However, whether or not mental imagery has functional importance for cognition has been the subject of a long-standing debate (see Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). One way to gain insight into the relationship between mental imagery and cognitive processes is to investigate how individual differences in mental imagery abilities relate to more direct indices of cognitive performance. To gauge imagery strength, researchers have taken to collecting trial-by-trial self-reports of vividness or alternatively have used tools such as the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023) to quantify trait differences in mental imagery. Trial-to-trial variation in self-reported vividness ratings have been found to correlate with the number of visual details reported in autobiographical memories (D’Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), the degree of neural reinstatement during memory recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015) and the degree of neural overlap between imagery and perception (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). In addition, VVIQ scores predict the degree to which an mental imagery induces priming during binocular rivalry tasks (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011); and the size of pupillary light response when imagining bright versus dim stimuli (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). Thus, there is ample evidence suggesting that self-reported measures of mental imagery strength relates to neural and behavioral measures of cognitive performance in memory and perception tasks.
Interestingly, these self-reports have revealed individuals who have little to no experience with mental images. Cases of a lack of imagery have been reported since the 19th century (Galton, 1880), a condition now referred to as Aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015). Aphantasia is relatively rare, with recent reports indicating a prevalence rate as low as 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to as high as 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia (henceforth referred to as aphantasics) often interpret the phrase “seeing something in your mind” as a metaphor, and are surprised to learn that others actually report experiencing something more literally image-like (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). On the surface, aphantasics lead normal lives, notwithstanding the evidence reviewed above suggesting that mental imagery plays a role in a number of cognitive activities. How might their lack of mental imagery affect their cognition? 
When interviewed about their autobiographical memory, aphantasics report fewer episodic and visual details, with this effect being more prominent when they are asked to simulate hypothetical future events (Alexei J. Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Alexei J. Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020) suggesting a connection to a relative phemonon referred to as “severely deficient autobiographical memory” (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018).  Aphantasics do, however, report having dreams, suggesting that they may retain unconscious forms of mental imagery (Zeman et al., 2020), although it has been reported that aphantasics dream less frequently than controls and that these dreams tend to be less vivid (Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020). While the occurrence of unconscious mental imagery might suggest that aphantasia results from a failure of metacognition, behavioural studies have shown that they neither exhibit imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018), nor a change in the pupillary light response when imagining stimuli of different brightness (Kay et al., 2022). These findings have been taken to suggest that their mental representations indeed lack sensory and phenomenal properties. Taken together, the above-reviewed research suggests that aphantasics tend to process visual information at higher levels of abstraction. Indeed, when performing visual working memory tasks, aphantasics do as well as controls, but report having used strategies  that make use of semantic labels (Keogh et al., 2021) rather than lower level sensory information.
What neurological mechanisms might underlie aphantasia? Neuroimaging studies have demonstrated that the contents of their imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) can be decoded in the early visual cortex, suggesting content specific information is present in early visual cortex representations of aphantasics. However, the content of imagery could not be cross-decoded with activity from perception (Meng et al., 2023) suggesting that the information that aphantasics store in these representations appears to be different than their representations formed during perception. Additionally when aphantasics were asked to imagine a stimulus at a specific location, neural activity was greater in the ipsilateral hemisphere whereas in controls activity was greater in the contralateral hemisphere (Meng et al., 2023). Together these findings suggest that the imagery related representations of aphantasics either contains less—or transformed—sensory information compared to controls.
Another functional neuroimaging report showed that aphantasics exhibit reduced resting state connectivity between prefrontal cortices and visual cortices and less left anterior parietal activity when asked to actively visualize faces and scenes (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). Because mental imagery is believed to be supported by the top-down activation of sensory areas, it is thought to rely more heavily on recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices compared to perception (Nadine Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). Thus, the reduced connectivity might partly underlie the aphantasic inability to  voluntarily create mental images.
Similar findings were observed in an fMRI study examining the autobiographical memory deficits of aphantasics (Monzel et al., 2023). When asked to retrieve an autobiographical memory, aphantasics were shown to have reduced activation in bilateral hippocampus, increased activation of the visual cortices, and reduced connectivity between hippocampus and visual cortices. This increased activation of visual cortices during memory retrieval was particularly interesting, and suggested that the deficits to autobiographical memory may be related to a reduced signal-to-noise ratio caused by the hyper activity of visual cortices (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023).
In this paper, we ask how perceptual pattern completion, another process theorized to depend on recurrent connections (Rolls, 2013), might be impacted in aphantasia. Perceptual pattern completion enables us to recognize a partially degraded or occluded object. Activity from the partial object is thought to spread to the missing features through both lateral and vertical recurrent connections (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), and this has been supported with evidence from computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). This recurrent processing can be inhibited by backward masking stimuli with visual noise, impairing recognition (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O’Reilly, 2012). When a partial stimulus is viewed, higher areas process the resulting feedforward signals, forming predictions about the identity of the stimulus and send top-down feedback signals to lower areas, strengthening and reinforcing neural responses in lower areas. However, when a mask quickly follows the stimulus as in backward masking, feedforward signals from the mask travel up the processing stream at the same time as the feedback prediction signals related to the previous stimulus. This mismatch between feedforward and feedback signals is thought to be the cause of the observed impairments to perception by backward masking (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).
How might pattern completion in aphantasics be affected by visual disruption via backward masking? Backward masking is believed to selectively disrupt recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000), and because aphantasics have reduced connectivity between frontoparietal and visual cortices (Milton et al., 2021), they might be more heavily impacted by disruption and thus show difficulty in recognizing a partial stimulus when it is presented briefly and followed by a mask. However, aphantasics tend to favour semantic strategies when completing visual tasks (Keogh et al., 2021) and their imagery related presentations appear to have less sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023). It therefore may be the case that aphantasics are able to perform a kind of high-level pattern completion by relying on the retrieval of semantic details when presented with a partial visual stimulus. Thus, an alternative hypothesis is that, due to aphantasics’ tendency to process stimuli at a higher level of abstraction (and therefore not rely on top-down inference of low-level details), backward masking might have a reduced impact on their ability to recognize the partial stimulus. In the present study, we test these alternative hypotheses, in a group of self-identified aphantasics and matched controls that completed a task where we briefly presented partially occluded images depicting objects and asked participants to identify the object as belonging to one of four categories. Crucially, these partially occluded images were sometimes followed by a visual noise mask, aimed at disrupting recurrent processing that is thought to support pattern completion.

**Paragraph 4** revision:

**Current version:**
"When interviewed about their autobiographical memory, aphantasics report fewer episodic and visual details, with this effect being more prominent when they are asked to simulate hypothetical future events (Alexei J. Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Alexei J. Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020) suggesting a connection to a relative phemonon referred to as "severely deficient autobiographical memory" (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). Aphantasics do, however, report having dreams, suggesting that they may retain unconscious forms of mental imagery (Zeman et al., 2020), although it has been reported that aphantasics dream less frequently than controls and that these dreams tend to be less vivid (Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020). While the occurrence of unconscious mental imagery might suggest that aphantasia results from a failure of metacognition, behavioural studies have shown that they neither exhibit imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018), nor a change in the pupillary light response when imagining stimuli of different brightness (Kay et al., 2022). These findings have been taken to suggest that their mental representations indeed lack sensory and phenomenal properties. Taken together, the above-reviewed research suggests that aphantasics tend to process visual information at higher levels of abstraction. Indeed, when performing visual working memory tasks, aphantasics do as well as controls, but report having used strategies that make use of semantic labels (Keogh et al., 2021) rather than lower level sensory information."

**Suggested revision:**
"Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021)."

## Introduction

Mental imagery—the ability to generate internal visual representations without external stimulation—plays a fundamental role in human cognition. This capacity to "see with the mind's eye" supports diverse cognitive functions including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).

Despite its apparent importance, the functional role of mental imagery remains debated (Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). Individual differences in imagery vividness, measured through trial-by-trial reports or the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023), provide insight into this relationship. Self-reported vividness correlates with multiple cognitive and neural measures: visual details in autobiographical memories (D'Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), neural reinstatement during recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015), and imagery-perception neural overlap (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). VVIQ scores also predict imagery-induced priming in binocular rivalry (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011) and pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). These converging findings demonstrate that subjective imagery reports reliably index objective neurocognitive processes.

Remarkably, some individuals report little to no mental imagery experience—a condition termed aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015), first documented by Galton (1880). Prevalence estimates range from 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia often discover their difference only when learning that others experience literal mental images, having previously interpreted "seeing in the mind's eye" as metaphorical (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). Despite the apparent importance of imagery for cognition, these individuals lead typical lives, raising fundamental questions about how they accomplish imagery-dependent tasks.

Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

# Aphantasia and Visual Pattern Completion: Evidence for Fragile Recurrent Processing

## Abstract

Aphantasia is the inability to form voluntary mental images despite intact cognitive function. We hypothesized that aphantasia involves processing visual information at higher levels of abstraction, coupled with deficient recurrent feedback processes necessary for vivid imagery. To test whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to disruption of recurrent processing, we employed a perceptual pattern completion task with backward masking. Thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and 41 controls completed a task where they categorized briefly presented, partially occluded objects. Crucially, these images were followed by either visual noise masks (disrupting recurrent processing) or blank screens. Contrary to our initial hypothesis that aphantasics would be less affected by masking due to their reliance on semantic strategies, we found they were significantly more impacted by backward masking than controls. While both groups showed comparable accuracy without masking, the interaction between group and masking condition revealed greater vulnerability in aphantasics when recurrent processing was disrupted. After controlling for speed-accuracy trade-offs using the Balanced Integration Score, this interaction remained significant. These findings suggest that individuals with aphantasia possess intact but fragile recurrent processing, making them more susceptible to visual interference. This vulnerability may reflect the reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity previously observed in aphantasia, supporting the view that their imagery deficits stem from weakened top-down feedback mechanisms rather than their complete absence.

**Word count: 216**

## Keywords
aphantasia, mental imagery, recurrent processing, backward masking, pattern completion, visual perception, top-down feedback

## Introduction

content.text:
**Paragraph 5** revision:

**Current version:**
"What neurological mechanisms might underlie aphantasia? Neuroimaging studies have demonstrated that the contents of their imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) can be decoded in the early visual cortex, suggesting content specific information is present in early visual cortex representations of aphantasics. However, the content of imagery could not be cross-decoded with activity from perception (Meng et al., 2023) suggesting that the information that aphantasics store in these representations appears to be different than their representations formed during perception. Additionally when aphantasics were asked to imagine a stimulus at a specific location, neural activity was greater in the ipsilateral hemisphere whereas in controls activity was greater in the contralateral hemisphere (Meng et al., 2023). Together these findings suggest that the imagery related representations of aphantasics either contains less—or transformed—sensory information compared to controls."

**Suggested revision:**
"Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail."

**Improvements:**
1. Much more concise (reduced from 139 to 94 words)
2. Stronger opening that highlights the paradox
3. Fixed grammar ("different than" → implied "different from")
4. Eliminated awkward phrasing ("the information that aphantasics store in these representations appears to be")
5. More elegant explanation of the lateralization finding
6. Clearer concluding interpretation

Do you approve this revision?

input.new_string:
Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

Despite its apparent importance, the functional role of mental imagery remains debated (Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). Individual differences in imagery vividness, measured through trial-by-trial reports or the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023), provide insight into this relationship. Self-reported vividness correlates with multiple cognitive and neural measures: visual details in autobiographical memories (D'Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), neural reinstatement during recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015), and imagery-perception neural overlap (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). VVIQ scores also predict imagery-induced priming in binocular rivalry (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011) and pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). These converging findings demonstrate that subjective imagery reports reliably index objective neurocognitive processes.

Remarkably, some individuals report little to no mental imagery experience—a condition termed aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015), first documented by Galton (1880). Prevalence estimates range from 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia often discover their difference only when learning that others experience literal mental images, having previously interpreted "seeing in the mind's eye" as metaphorical (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). Despite the apparent importance of imagery for cognition, these individuals lead typical lives, raising fundamental questions about how they accomplish imagery-dependent tasks.

Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. +Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. The weakened connectivity likely explains the characteristic inability to voluntarily generate mental images in aphantasia.

# Aphantasia and Visual Pattern Completion: Evidence for Fragile Recurrent Processing

## Abstract

Aphantasia is the inability to form voluntary mental images despite intact cognitive function. We hypothesized that aphantasia involves processing visual information at higher levels of abstraction, coupled with deficient recurrent feedback processes necessary for vivid imagery. To test whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to disruption of recurrent processing, we employed a perceptual pattern completion task with backward masking. Thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and 41 controls completed a task where they categorized briefly presented, partially occluded objects. Crucially, these images were followed by either visual noise masks (disrupting recurrent processing) or blank screens. Contrary to our initial hypothesis that aphantasics would be less affected by masking due to their reliance on semantic strategies, we found they were significantly more impacted by backward masking than controls. While both groups showed comparable accuracy without masking, the interaction between group and masking condition revealed greater vulnerability in aphantasics when recurrent processing was disrupted. After controlling for speed-accuracy trade-offs using the Balanced Integration Score, this interaction remained significant. These findings suggest that individuals with aphantasia possess intact but fragile recurrent processing, making them more susceptible to visual interference. This vulnerability may reflect the reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity previously observed in aphantasia, supporting the view that their imagery deficits stem from weakened top-down feedback mechanisms rather than their complete absence.

**Word count: 216**

## Keywords
aphantasia, mental imagery, recurrent processing, backward masking, pattern completion, visual perception, top-down feedback

## Introduction

Mental imagery—the ability to generate internal visual representations without external stimulation—plays a fundamental role in human cognition. This capacity to "see with the mind's eye" supports diverse cognitive functions including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).

Despite its apparent importance, the functional role of mental imagery remains debated (Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). Individual differences in imagery vividness, measured through trial-by-trial reports or the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023), provide insight into this relationship. Self-reported vividness correlates with multiple cognitive and neural measures: visual details in autobiographical memories (D'Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), neural reinstatement during recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015), and imagery-perception neural overlap (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). VVIQ scores also predict imagery-induced priming in binocular rivalry (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011) and pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). These converging findings demonstrate that subjective imagery reports reliably index objective neurocognitive processes.

Remarkably, some individuals report little to no mental imagery experience—a condition termed aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015), first documented by Galton (1880). Prevalence estimates range from 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia often discover their difference only when learning that others experience literal mental images, having previously interpreted "seeing in the mind's eye" as metaphorical (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). Despite the apparent importance of imagery for cognition, these individuals lead typical lives, raising fundamental questions about how they accomplish imagery-dependent tasks.

Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

+Beyond representational differences, aphantasia involves altered connectivity patterns. Resting-state and task-based neuroimaging reveal reduced connectivity between prefrontal and visual cortices, alongside diminished left anterior parietal activity during visualization attempts (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). This pattern aligns with theoretical models positing that mental imagery requires stronger recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices than does perception alone (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). The weakened connectivity likely explains the characteristic inability to voluntarily generate mental images in aphantasia.

Excellent! Paragraph 6 has been added. Now let's continue with **Paragraph 7**:

**Current version:**
"Similar findings were observed in an fMRI study examining the autobiographical memory deficits of aphantasics (Monzel et al., 2023). When asked to retrieve an autobiographical memory, aphantasics were shown to have reduced activation in bilateral hippocampus, increased activation of the visual cortices, and reduced connectivity between hippocampus and visual cortices. This increased activation of visual cortices during memory retrieval was particularly interesting, and suggested that the deficits to autobiographical memory may be related to a reduced signal-to-noise ratio caused by the hyper activity of visual cortices (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023)."

**Suggested revision:**
"These connectivity alterations extend to memory systems. During autobiographical memory retrieval, individuals with aphantasia show a paradoxical pattern: reduced hippocampal activation and hippocampal-visual connectivity, yet increased visual cortex activity (Monzel et al., 2023). This hyperactivity in visual regions during memory retrieval may reflect compensatory processes or, alternatively, a reduced signal-to-noise ratio that impairs the reinstatement of sensory details (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023). Such findings further support the view that aphantasia involves disrupted communication between memory and sensory systems."

**Improvements:**
1. Better topic sentence that connects to previous paragraph about connectivity
2. More concise (reduced from 92 to 83 words)
3. Clearer explanation of the paradox
4. Removed redundant phrases ("was particularly interesting, and suggested")
5. Added interpretation about what this means for the broader understanding
6. Better flow into the next section about pattern completion

Prevalence estimates range from 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia often discover their difference only when learning that others experience literal mental images, having previously interpreted "seeing in the mind's eye" as metaphorical (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). Despite the apparent importance of imagery for cognition, these individuals lead typical lives, raising fundamental questions about how they accomplish imagery-dependent tasks.

Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

Beyond representational differences, aphantasia involves altered connectivity patterns. Resting-state and task-based neuroimaging reveal reduced connectivity between prefrontal and visual cortices, alongside diminished left anterior parietal activity during visualization attempts (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). This pattern aligns with theoretical models positing that mental imagery requires stronger recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices than does perception alone (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). The weakened connectivity likely explains the characteristic inability to voluntarily generate mental images in aphantasia.

Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

Beyond representational differences, aphantasia involves altered connectivity patterns. Resting-state and task-based neuroimaging reveal reduced connectivity between prefrontal and visual cortices, alongside diminished left anterior parietal activity during visualization attempts (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). This pattern aligns with theoretical models positing that mental imagery requires stronger recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices than does perception alone (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). The weakened connectivity likely explains the characteristic inability to voluntarily generate mental images in aphantasia.

+These connectivity alterations extend to memory systems. During autobiographical memory retrieval, individuals with aphantasia show a paradoxical pattern: reduced hippocampal activation and hippocampal-visual connectivity, yet increased visual cortex activity (Monzel et al., 2023). This hyperactivity in visual regions during memory retrieval may reflect compensatory processes or, alternatively, a reduced signal-to-noise ratio that impairs the reinstatement of sensory details (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023). Such findings further support the view that aphantasia involves disrupted communication between memory and sensory systems.

Great! Paragraph 7 has been added. Now let's move to **Paragraph 8**, which introduces the concept of pattern completion and backward masking:

**Current version:**
"In this paper, we ask how perceptual pattern completion, another process theorized to depend on recurrent connections (Rolls, 2013), might be impacted in aphantasia. Perceptual pattern completion enables us to recognize a partially degraded or occluded object. Activity from the partial object is thought to spread to the missing features through both lateral and vertical recurrent connections (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), and this has been supported with evidence from computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). This recurrent processing can be inhibited by backward masking stimuli with visual noise, impairing recognition (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O'Reilly, 2012). When a partial stimulus is viewed, higher areas process the resulting feedforward signals, forming predictions about the identity of the stimulus and send top-down feedback signals to lower areas, strengthening and reinforcing neural responses in lower areas. However, when a mask quickly follows the stimulus as in backward masking, feedforward signals from the mask travel up the processing stream at the same time as the feedback prediction signals related to the previous stimulus. This mismatch between feedforward and feedback signals is thought to be the cause of the observed impairments to perception by backward masking (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000)."

**Suggested revision:**
"Given these widespread connectivity disruptions, we investigated whether perceptual pattern completion—another recurrent-dependent process (Rolls, 2013)—is compromised in aphantasia. Pattern completion allows recognition of partially occluded objects through recurrent connections that spread activation from visible features to missing ones (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), a mechanism supported by computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). Critically, this process can be selectively disrupted using backward masking, where visual noise presented shortly after a target stimulus interferes with recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O'Reilly, 2012). The mechanism involves temporal conflict: feedforward signals from the mask ascend the visual hierarchy simultaneously with descending feedback about the target, disrupting the iterative refinement necessary for pattern completion (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000)."

**Improvements:**
1. Much more concise (reduced from 188 to 124 words)
2. Better transition from previous paragraphs about connectivity
3. Clearer explanation of the mechanism without losing technical accuracy
4. Eliminated redundancy in explaining the process
5. Sets up the experimental approach more directly

Do you approve this revision?

# Aphantasia and Visual Pattern Completion: Evidence for Fragile Recurrent Processing

## Abstract

Aphantasia is the inability to form voluntary mental images despite intact cognitive function. We hypothesized that aphantasia involves processing visual information at higher levels of abstraction, coupled with deficient recurrent feedback processes necessary for vivid imagery. To test whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to disruption of recurrent processing, we employed a perceptual pattern completion task with backward masking. Thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and 41 controls completed a task where they categorized briefly presented, partially occluded objects. Crucially, these images were followed by either visual noise masks (disrupting recurrent processing) or blank screens. Contrary to our initial hypothesis that aphantasics would be less affected by masking due to their reliance on semantic strategies, we found they were significantly more impacted by backward masking than controls. While both groups showed comparable accuracy without masking, the interaction between group and masking condition revealed greater vulnerability in aphantasics when recurrent processing was disrupted. After controlling for speed-accuracy trade-offs using the Balanced Integration Score, this interaction remained significant. These findings suggest that individuals with aphantasia possess intact but fragile recurrent processing, making them more susceptible to visual interference. This vulnerability may reflect the reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity previously observed in aphantasia, supporting the view that their imagery deficits stem from weakened top-down feedback mechanisms rather than their complete absence.

**Word count: 216**

## Keywords
aphantasia, mental imagery, recurrent processing, backward masking, pattern completion, visual perception, top-down feedback

## Introduction

Mental imagery—the ability to generate internal visual representations without external stimulation—plays a fundamental role in human cognition. This capacity to "see with the mind's eye" supports diverse cognitive functions including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).

Despite its apparent importance, the functional role of mental imagery remains debated (Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). Individual differences in imagery vividness, measured through trial-by-trial reports or the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023), provide insight into this relationship. Self-reported vividness correlates with multiple cognitive and neural measures: visual details in autobiographical memories (D'Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), neural reinstatement during recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015), and imagery-perception neural overlap (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). VVIQ scores also predict imagery-induced priming in binocular rivalry (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011) and pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). These converging findings demonstrate that subjective imagery reports reliably index objective neurocognitive processes.

Remarkably, some individuals report little to no mental imagery experience—a condition termed aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015), first documented by Galton (1880). Prevalence estimates range from 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia often discover their difference only when learning that others experience literal mental images, having previously interpreted "seeing in the mind's eye" as metaphorical (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). Despite the apparent importance of imagery for cognition, these individuals lead typical lives, raising fundamental questions about how they accomplish imagery-dependent tasks.

Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

Beyond representational differences, aphantasia involves altered connectivity patterns. Resting-state and task-based neuroimaging reveal reduced connectivity between prefrontal and visual cortices, alongside diminished left anterior parietal activity during visualization attempts (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). This pattern aligns with theoretical models positing that mental imagery requires stronger recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices than does perception alone (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). The weakened connectivity likely explains the characteristic inability to voluntarily generate mental images in aphantasia.

These connectivity alterations extend to memory systems. During autobiographical memory retrieval, individuals with aphantasia show a paradoxical pattern: reduced hippocampal activation and hippocampal-visual connectivity, yet increased visual cortex activity (Monzel et al., 2023). This hyperactivity in visual regions during memory retrieval may reflect compensatory processes or, alternatively, a reduced signal-to-noise ratio that impairs the reinstatement of sensory details (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023). Such findings further support the view that aphantasia involves disrupted communication between memory and sensory systems.

Such findings further support the view that aphantasia involves disrupted communication between memory and sensory systems.

+Given these widespread connectivity disruptions, we investigated whether perceptual pattern completion—another recurrent-dependent process (Rolls, 2013)—is compromised in aphantasia. Pattern completion allows recognition of partially occluded objects through recurrent connections that spread activation from visible features to missing ones (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), a mechanism supported by computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). The mechanism involves temporal conflict: feedforward signals from the mask ascend the visual hierarchy simultaneously with descending feedback about the target, disrupting the iterative refinement necessary for pattern completion (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).

content.text:
Excellent! Paragraph 8 has been added. Now let's work on the final paragraph of the introduction, **Paragraph 9**, which presents the competing hypotheses and study overview:

**Current version:**
"How might pattern completion in aphantasics be affected by visual disruption via backward masking? Backward masking is believed to selectively disrupt recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000), and because aphantasics have reduced connectivity between frontoparietal and visual cortices (Milton et al., 2021), they might be more heavily impacted by disruption and thus show difficulty in recognizing a partial stimulus when it is presented briefly and followed by a mask. However, aphantasics tend to favour semantic strategies when completing visual tasks (Keogh et al., 2021) and their imagery related presentations appear to have less sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023). It therefore may be the case that aphantasics are able to perform a kind of high-level pattern completion by relying on the retrieval of semantic details when presented with a partial visual stimulus. Thus, an alternative hypothesis is that, due to aphantasics' tendency to process stimuli at a higher level of abstraction (and therefore not rely on top-down inference of low-level details), backward masking might have a reduced impact on their ability to recognize the partial stimulus. In the present study, we test these alternative hypotheses, in a group of self-identified aphantasics and matched controls that completed a task where we briefly presented partially occluded images depicting objects and asked participants to identify the object as belonging to one of four categories. Crucially, these partially occluded images were sometimes followed by a visual noise mask, aimed at disrupting recurrent processing that is thought to support pattern completion."

**Suggested revision:**
"This paradigm offers a unique window into aphantasia's perceptual consequences. Two competing hypotheses emerge from the literature. First, given their reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Milton et al., 2021), individuals with aphantasia might show greater vulnerability to backward masking, struggling more than controls when recurrent processing is disrupted. Alternatively, their reliance on semantic rather than sensory strategies (Keogh et al., 2021) and representations lacking sensory detail (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023) might render them less dependent on recurrent processing, thus paradoxically protecting them from masking effects. To test these hypotheses, we presented thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and matched controls with briefly displayed, partially occluded objects for categorization. Critical trials included backward masks designed to disrupt the recurrent processing necessary for pattern completion, allowing us to assess whether aphantasia involves fundamentally altered perceptual mechanisms."

**Improvements:**
1. Much more concise (reduced from 228 to 132 words)
2. Clearer presentation of the two hypotheses as a direct contrast
3. Removed redundancy and repetition
4. More elegant transition to methods
5. Stronger concluding sentence that emphasizes the theoretical importance

Do you approve this revision?

Critically, this process can be selectively disrupted using backward masking, where visual noise presented shortly after a target stimulus interferes with recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O'Reilly, 2012). The mechanism involves temporal conflict: feedforward signals from the mask ascend the visual hierarchy simultaneously with descending feedback about the target, disrupting the iterative refinement necessary for pattern completion (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).

Given these widespread connectivity disruptions, we investigated whether perceptual pattern completion—another recurrent-dependent process (Rolls, 2013)—is compromised in aphantasia. Pattern completion allows recognition of partially occluded objects through recurrent connections that spread activation from visible features to missing ones (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), a mechanism supported by computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). Critically, this process can be selectively disrupted using backward masking, where visual noise presented shortly after a target stimulus interferes with recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O'Reilly, 2012). The mechanism involves temporal conflict: feedforward signals from the mask ascend the visual hierarchy simultaneously with descending feedback about the target, disrupting the iterative refinement necessary for pattern completion (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).

This paradigm offers a unique window into aphantasia's perceptual consequences. Two competing hypotheses emerge from the literature. First, given their reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Milton et al., 2021), individuals with aphantasia might show greater vulnerability to backward masking, struggling more than controls when recurrent processing is disrupted. Alternatively, their reliance on semantic rather than sensory strategies (Keogh et al., 2021) and representations lacking sensory detail (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023) might render them less dependent on recurrent processing, thus paradoxically protecting them from masking effects. To test these hypotheses, we presented thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and matched controls with briefly displayed, partially occluded objects for categorization. Critical trials included backward masks designed to disrupt the recurrent processing necessary for pattern completion, allowing us to assess whether aphantasia involves fundamentally altered perceptual mechanisms.

# Aphantasia and Visual Pattern Completion: Evidence for Fragile Recurrent Processing

## Abstract

Aphantasia is the inability to form voluntary mental images despite intact cognitive function. We hypothesized that aphantasia involves processing visual information at higher levels of abstraction, coupled with deficient recurrent feedback processes necessary for vivid imagery. To test whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to disruption of recurrent processing, we employed a perceptual pattern completion task with backward masking. Thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and 41 controls completed a task where they categorized briefly presented, partially occluded objects. Crucially, these images were followed by either visual noise masks (disrupting recurrent processing) or blank screens. Contrary to our initial hypothesis that aphantasics would be less affected by masking due to their reliance on semantic strategies, we found they were significantly more impacted by backward masking than controls. While both groups showed comparable accuracy without masking, the interaction between group and masking condition revealed greater vulnerability in aphantasics when recurrent processing was disrupted. After controlling for speed-accuracy trade-offs using the Balanced Integration Score, this interaction remained significant. These findings suggest that individuals with aphantasia possess intact but fragile recurrent processing, making them more susceptible to visual interference. This vulnerability may reflect the reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity previously observed in aphantasia, supporting the view that their imagery deficits stem from weakened top-down feedback mechanisms rather than their complete absence.

**Word count: 216**

## Keywords
aphantasia, mental imagery, recurrent processing, backward masking, pattern completion, visual perception, top-down feedback

## Introduction

Mental imagery—the ability to generate internal visual representations without external stimulation—plays a fundamental role in human cognition. This capacity to "see with the mind's eye" supports diverse cognitive functions including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).

Despite its apparent importance, the functional role of mental imagery remains debated (Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). Individual differences in imagery vividness, measured through trial-by-trial reports or the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023), provide insight into this relationship. Self-reported vividness correlates with multiple cognitive and neural measures: visual details in autobiographical memories (D'Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), neural reinstatement during recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015), and imagery-perception neural overlap (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). VVIQ scores also predict imagery-induced priming in binocular rivalry (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011) and pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). These converging findings demonstrate that subjective imagery reports reliably index objective neurocognitive processes.

Remarkably, some individuals report little to no mental imagery experience—a condition termed aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015), first documented by Galton (1880). Prevalence estimates range from 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia often discover their difference only when learning that others experience literal mental images, having previously interpreted "seeing in the mind's eye" as metaphorical (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). Despite the apparent importance of imagery for cognition, these individuals lead typical lives, raising fundamental questions about how they accomplish imagery-dependent tasks.

Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

Beyond representational differences, aphantasia involves altered connectivity patterns. Resting-state and task-based neuroimaging reveal reduced connectivity between prefrontal and visual cortices, alongside diminished left anterior parietal activity during visualization attempts (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). This pattern aligns with theoretical models positing that mental imagery requires stronger recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices than does perception alone (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). The weakened connectivity likely explains the characteristic inability to voluntarily generate mental images in aphantasia.

These connectivity alterations extend to memory systems. During autobiographical memory retrieval, individuals with aphantasia show a paradoxical pattern: reduced hippocampal activation and hippocampal-visual connectivity, yet increased visual cortex activity (Monzel et al., 2023). This hyperactivity in visual regions during memory retrieval may reflect compensatory processes or, alternatively, a reduced signal-to-noise ratio that impairs the reinstatement of sensory details (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023). Such findings further support the view that aphantasia involves disrupted communication between memory and sensory systems.

Given these widespread connectivity disruptions, we investigated whether perceptual pattern completion—another recurrent-dependent process (Rolls, 2013)—is compromised in aphantasia. Pattern completion allows recognition of partially occluded objects through recurrent connections that spread activation from visible features to missing ones (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), a mechanism supported by computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). Critically, this process can be selectively disrupted using backward masking, where visual noise presented shortly after a target stimulus interferes with recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O'Reilly, 2012). The mechanism involves temporal conflict: feedforward signals from the mask ascend the visual hierarchy simultaneously with descending feedback about the target, disrupting the iterative refinement necessary for pattern completion (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).
