Discussion
Here we asked if the ability of aphantasics to identify the category of an image from a partial cue would be differentially affected by backward masking with visual noise compared to controls.  At the outset we proposed two alternative hypothesis. The first is that aphantasics, due to their hypothesized deficit in to-down reactivation of sensory properties of object represenations (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021), would be more heavily impacted by visual disruption and thus show difficulty in recognizing a partial stimulus when it is presented briefly and followed by a mask. Alternatively, we proposed that aphantasics would be less impacted by visual disruption owing to the tendency of aphantasics to process stimuli at a higher level of abstraction (and therefore not rely on top-down inference of low-level details)(Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Keogh et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023). In support of the first hypothesis, we found that aphantasics were able to successfully categorize objects from the brief presentation of an occluded image, and this ability was significantly reduced, compared to controls, when the cue was followed by the presentation of a visual noise mask. Thus, the latter of the two hypotheses was supported.
The finding that aphantasics were more vulnerable to backward masking compared to controls is revealing, as it suggests that aphantasics may still rely on recurrent processing, but that it is less robust, and therefore more susceptible to backward masking than in control participants. The finding is also consistent with functional neuroimaging, where aphantasics have been shown to have reduced, but not absent, connectivity between frontoparietal executive cortices and visual cortices (Liu, Cohen, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021) and between hippocampus and visual cortices (Monzel et al., 2023). This weaker recurrent connectivity is believed to be the basis for their inability to voluntarily initiate mental imagery (Nadine Dijkstra et al., 2019). Recurrent communication between executive and visual areas is also thought to support perception and pattern completion (N. Dijkstra, Zeidman, Ondobaka, Van Gerven, & Friston, 2017; Tang et al., 2018). Higher order executive regions are thought to identify features from the partial information provided by sensory visual regions. These higher regions then form predictive signals which are sent back to the sensory visual regions which are used to strengthen and prioritize patterns that match these predictions. The predictive signals are thought in turn to create prediction errors, which are then sent back to higher regions (Fyall et al., 2017; Shin et al., 2023). It is this interplay between top-down and bottom-up signals that allows for accurate perception (Den Ouden, Kok, & de Lange, 2012). However, according to this line of thought, it may be that because communication along these pathways is weaker in those with aphantasia, the resulting representations are relatively fragile, and thus more susceptible to visual disruption via backward masking.
Mental imagery, as defined in a review by Pearson and colleagues (Pearson, Naselaris, Holmes, & Kosslyn, 2015), is the representation of sensory information without a direct external stimulus. Under this definition, the retrieval and representation of missing portions of an occluded image via pattern completion can be considered a form of mental imagery, as the represented missing portions are not present in the external stimulus. But given the evidence that the imagery of aphantasics lacks sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023), how is it that aphantasics are able to complete the task? One possibility is that aphantasics have a form of intact unconscious mental imagery that occurs outside their awareness or control. In support of this idea, some aphantasics report having vivid sleeping dreams (Zeman et al., 2020), though use of the term “unconscious” in this manner is debated due to the phenomenological nature of dreams (see Nanay, 2021). Thus, while aphantasics may not be able to voluntarily evoke an image of a beach, top-down visual processes that occur outside of conscious awareness of may still be intact. It should be noted that not all individuals report having dreams, with some indicating they do not experience mental imagery at all, conscious or unconscious. Although, it is be possible that the majority of the aphantasics in the current study had intact unconscious mental imagery, it is not currently clear how to reliably measure this construct in a way that clearly dissociates from conscious imagery.
One thing to note about our results is the lack of within group relationship between VVIQ scores and task performance (categorization of partial images). Given that the VVIQ is thought to measure individual differences in imagery abilities, we would expect that variance in VVIQ scores should correlate with task performance and recurrent processing capability in our control group. Thus, our results raise questions about the construct validity of the VVIQ. Admittedly, our inability to detect a relationship between VVIQ scores and categorization performance may reflect a lack of statistical power; however, concerns over the mixed results on the validity of the VVIQ have been previously raised (Chara & Hamm, 1989) with a recent meta-analysis demonstrating that use of trial-by-trial vividness reports provide higher predictive value than VVIQ scores (Runge, Bakhilau, Omer, & D’Angiulli, 2015). A related issue is that there is little variation in the VVIQ scores of our sample of Aphantasics and samples of other studies. This is partly by design as researchers commonly use a narrow range of scores to diagnose or verify individuals with aphantasia (a range of 16-32 is often used for the VVIQ or 32-64 for the VVIQ2). Despite this, recent surveys suggest that there is a spectrum of imagery experience amongst those with Aphantasia (Zeman et al., 2020).  The limited variation in VVIQ scores among aphantasics may reduce our ability to detect their individual differences in imaging abilities. For example, VVIQ scores predict the degree to which mental imagery affects visual priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) and the pupillary light response (Kay et al., 2022) in control participants. Meanwhile, there is no relationship between VVIQ scores and these behavioral measures in aphantasics. Similarly, we find no relationship between VVIQ scores and categorization performance in our Aphantasic group. Together these results suggest that additional tools or measures are needed to help examine individual differences in imagery in aphantasia.
 A related concern is the heterogeneous nature of aphantasia. With the accumulation of anecdotal and experimental evidence, it is becoming apparent that there exists a wide spectrum of experience with mental imagery among those with aphantasia. Aphantasia does not only describe a complete absence of mental imagery, but also includes those with dim or vague imagery. As mentioned earlier there are individual differences in the ability to dream, and among those who are able to dream, the quality of those dreams varies as well. For some individuals dreams occur infrequently and are visually impoverished compared to controls (Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020; Milton et al., 2021), while others report experiencing highly vivid dreams (Zeman et al., 2020). Recent reports find that individuals with aphantasia tend to be affected in multiple modalities (Dance, Ward, & Simner, 2021; Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020; Alexei Joel Dawes, Keogh, & Pearson, 2023) with 97% of the surveyed sample reporting similar weak imagery in at least one other non-imagery modality, and 62% reporting weak (absent or vague/dim) imagery in all modalities (tactile, gustatory, auditory, bodily, olfactory, movement). While the multimodal nature of aphantasia may not directly speak to the current study, it does suggest a direction of future research on aphantasia as a whole. For example, if recurrent connectivity during mental imagery is generally deficient in aphantasics, the kind of effect we observed in the present study should be reproduced in other modalities. Existing studies have utilized the VVIQ to gauge mental imagery, which primarily measures vividness in the visual domain. Thus, future studies would benefit from expanding scale ranges or developing paradigms that allow for a wider range of imagery experience to be considered, including those in other modalities.
In conclusion, aphantasia describes the condition in which one is unable to voluntarily form vivid mental images. These deficits in imagery are thought to be related to a reduced ability to reinstate perceptual activity in early visual regions via top-down mediation. Supporting this idea, aphantasics have been shown to haveweaker connectivity between frontoparietal executive and visual cortices.  Pattern completion of partial objects is also thought to rely on recurrent connectivity between these regions and this recurrent processing can be probed with the rapid presentation of a backward mask. Here we asked if pattern completion in aphantasics would be differentially impacted by backward masking with visual noise. We had two alternative hypothesis predicting that the ability of aphantasics to categorize partial images would be relatively undisrupted due to their preference for semantic strategies or that they would be more vulnerable to visual disruption due to their weaker recurrent connectivity. Our findings were in line with our second hypothesis, aphantasics were more negatively impacted by visual noise than controls. Our findings suggests that aphantasics have weak, but nevertheless, intact recurrent processing that is more vulnerable to visual interference.

input.old_string: ## Discussion
input.new_string:
## Discussion

We examined whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to backward masking during pattern completion. Two competing hypotheses emerged: first, that reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021) would render aphantasics more vulnerable to visual disruption; alternatively, that their reliance on semantic rather than sensory processing strategies (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Keogh et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023) might protect them from masking effects. Our findings supported the first hypothesis: while individuals with aphantasia successfully categorized partially occluded objects, they showed significantly greater impairment than controls when visual noise masks disrupted recurrent processing.

Two competing hypotheses emerged: first, that reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021) would render aphantasics more vulnerable to visual disruption; alternatively, that their reliance on semantic rather than sensory processing strategies (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Keogh et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023) might protect them from masking effects. toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Aphantasia and Visual Pattern Completion: Evidence for Fragile Recurrent Processing

## Abstract

Aphantasia is the inability to form voluntary mental images despite intact cognitive function. We hypothesized that aphantasia involves processing visual information at higher levels of abstraction, coupled with deficient recurrent feedback processes necessary for vivid imagery. To test whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to disruption of recurrent processing, we employed a perceptual pattern completion task with backward masking. Thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and 41 controls completed a task where they categorized briefly presented, partially occluded objects. Crucially, these images were followed by either visual noise masks (disrupting recurrent processing) or blank screens. Contrary to our initial hypothesis that aphantasics would be less affected by masking due to their reliance on semantic strategies, we found they were significantly more impacted by backward masking than controls. While both groups showed comparable accuracy without masking, the interaction between group and masking condition revealed greater vulnerability in aphantasics when recurrent processing was disrupted. After controlling for speed-accuracy trade-offs using the Balanced Integration Score, this interaction remained significant. These findings suggest that individuals with aphantasia possess intact but fragile recurrent processing, making them more susceptible to visual interference. This vulnerability may reflect the reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity previously observed in aphantasia, supporting the view that their imagery deficits stem from weakened top-down feedback mechanisms rather than their complete absence.

**Word count: 216**

## Keywords
aphantasia, mental imagery, recurrent processing, backward masking, pattern completion, visual perception, top-down feedback

## Introduction

Mental imagery—the ability to generate internal visual representations without external stimulation—plays a fundamental role in human cognition. This capacity to "see with the mind's eye" supports diverse cognitive functions including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).

Despite its apparent importance, the functional role of mental imagery remains debated (Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). Individual differences in imagery vividness, measured through trial-by-trial reports or the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023), provide insight into this relationship. Self-reported vividness correlates with multiple cognitive and neural measures: visual details in autobiographical memories (D'Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), neural reinstatement during recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015), and imagery-perception neural overlap (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). VVIQ scores also predict imagery-induced priming in binocular rivalry (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011) and pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). These converging findings demonstrate that subjective imagery reports reliably index objective neurocognitive processes.

Remarkably, some individuals report little to no mental imagery experience—a condition termed aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015), first documented by Galton (1880). Prevalence estimates range from 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia often discover their difference only when learning that others experience literal mental images, having previously interpreted "seeing in the mind's eye" as metaphorical (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). Despite the apparent importance of imagery for cognition, these individuals lead typical lives, raising fundamental questions about how they accomplish imagery-dependent tasks.

Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

Beyond representational differences, aphantasia involves altered connectivity patterns. Resting-state and task-based neuroimaging reveal reduced connectivity between prefrontal and visual cortices, alongside diminished left anterior parietal activity during visualization attempts (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). This pattern aligns with theoretical models positing that mental imagery requires stronger recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices than does perception alone (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). The weakened connectivity likely explains the characteristic inability to voluntarily generate mental images in aphantasia.

These connectivity alterations extend to memory systems. During autobiographical memory retrieval, individuals with aphantasia show a paradoxical pattern: reduced hippocampal activation and hippocampal-visual connectivity, yet increased visual cortex activity (Monzel et al., 2023). This hyperactivity in visual regions during memory retrieval may reflect compensatory processes or, alternatively, a reduced signal-to-noise ratio that impairs the reinstatement of sensory details (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023). Such findings further support the view that aphantasia involves disrupted communication between memory and sensory systems.

Given these widespread connectivity disruptions, we investigated whether perceptual pattern completion—another recurrent-dependent process (Rolls, 2013)—is compromised in aphantasia. Pattern completion allows recognition of partially occluded objects through recurrent connections that spread activation from visible features to missing ones (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), a mechanism supported by computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). Critically, this process can be selectively disrupted using backward masking, where visual noise presented shortly after a target stimulus interferes with recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O'Reilly, 2012). The mechanism involves temporal conflict: feedforward signals from the mask ascend the visual hierarchy simultaneously with descending feedback about the target, disrupting the iterative refinement necessary for pattern completion (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).

This paradigm offers a unique window into aphantasia's perceptual consequences. Two competing hypotheses emerge from the literature. First, given their reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Milton et al., 2021), individuals with aphantasia might show greater vulnerability to backward masking, struggling more than controls when recurrent processing is disrupted. Alternatively, their reliance on semantic rather than sensory strategies (Keogh et al., 2021) and representations lacking sensory detail (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023) might render them less dependent on recurrent processing, thus paradoxically protecting them from masking effects. To test these hypotheses, we presented thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and matched controls with briefly displayed, partially occluded objects for categorization. Critical trials included backward masks designed to disrupt the recurrent processing necessary for pattern completion, allowing us to assess whether aphantasia involves fundamentally altered perceptual mechanisms.

## Methods

### Participants

Thirty-nine individuals with aphantasia (aged 18-77) participated: 35 self-identified through Reddit and Facebook communities, plus 4 identified via VVIQ scores during control recruitment. Inclusion criteria required lifelong absence of voluntary mental imagery with no precipitating injury. Four participants were excluded for excessive non-responding or chance-level performance, yielding a final aphantasia sample of 35. The control group comprised 41 age-matched individuals (aged 18-77) recruited via Prolific (n=38), Reddit (n=2), and Facebook (n=1). All participants provided informed consent and received compensation for their time.

### Measures

Participants completed the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire 2 (VVIQ2; Marks, 1973), except for 17 controls who did not complete this measure. The VVIQ2 comprises 32 items across eight scenarios (e.g., driving, beach scenes) rated on a 5-point scale from 1 ("No image at all, you only 'know' that you are thinking of the object") to 5 ("Perfectly clear and as vivid as normal vision"). Lower scores indicate reduced imagery vividness.

### Stimuli

We used 320 grayscale images of objects from four categories (clothing, food, toys, and tools; see Figure 1). Images were sourced from Google image searches and resized to a maximum of 450 pixels (height or width) while preserving aspect ratios.

INSERT FIGURE 1 HERE

### Procedure

Participants completed a backward masking task (320 trials across two blocks) adapted from Tang et al. (2018; see Figure 2). Each trial followed a fixed sequence: 500 ms fixation, target image presentation (25, 50, 75, 100, or 150 ms), followed by either a visual noise mask or blank screen (500 ms), then a categorization response screen. Participants categorized objects into four groups (clothing, food, tools, toys) using keyboard responses 1-4.

INSERT FIGURE 2 HERE

Two image conditions were used: 25% intact images and 75% partially occluded images revealed through six Gaussian bubbles (σ = 26 pixels; Gosselin & Schyns, 2001). Masking occurred on 50% of trials (noise mask vs. blank screen). The design yielded 40 exemplars per category per block, with eight stimuli per duration level, counterbalanced across masking conditions.

## Results

### VVIQ Validation

We first verified that our groups differed in self-reported imagery vividness. VVIQ scores showed a bimodal distribution (Figure 3) with overall M = 73.53 (SD = 43.14, range = 32-160). Hartigan's dip test confirmed multimodality (Dip = 0.096, p < 0.001). The aphantasia group (M = 43.31, SD = 21.30, range = 32-90) scored significantly lower than controls (M = 119.52, SD = 21.45, range = 96-160), t(47) = 13.30, p < .0001, Cohen's d = 3.57.

INSERT FIGURE 3 HERE

### Categorization Performance

We examined whether backward masking differentially affected categorization performance between groups using linear mixed-effects models in R (lme4 package; Bates, Mächler, Bolker, & Walker, 2015) with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom approximation (lmerTest package; Kuznetsova, Brockhoff, & Christensen, 2017). Models included stimulus duration, masking condition, and group as fixed effects, with participant as a random effect. Separate analyses were conducted for intact and partially occluded images.

#### Intact Images

For unoccluded images (Figure 4), stimulus duration showed no significant effect (β = 0.00109, 95% CI [-0.00164, 0.00381], t = 0.78, p = 0.434; Std. β = 0.04, 95% CI [-0.06, 0.13]), with comparable performance between groups (β = -0.43, 95% CI [-0.96, 0.10], t = -1.60, p = 0.110; Std. β = -0.30, 95% CI [-0.65, 0.05]). Backward masking significantly reduced accuracy (β = -0.49, 95% CI [-0.84, -0.14], t = -2.76, p = 0.006; Std. β = -0.09, 95% CI [-0.22, 0.05]).

INSERT FIGURE 4 HERE

#### Partially Occluded Images

For partially occluded images (Figure 5), accuracy increased with longer stimulus durations (β = 0.00172, 95% CI [0.00140, 0.00204], t = 10.54, p < .001; Std. β = 0.45, 95% CI [0.37, 0.54]). Backward masking significantly reduced accuracy (β = -0.09, 95% CI [-0.13, -0.04], t = -4.09, p < .001; Std. β = -0.38, 95% CI [-0.50, -0.26]). The aphantasia group showed higher overall accuracy (β = 0.06, 95% CI [0.0008, 0.12], t = 1.99, p = 0.047; Std. β = 0.19, 95% CI [-0.10, 0.49]). Critically, a significant group × masking interaction emerged (β = -0.07, 95% CI [-0.13, -0.008], t = -2.21, p = 0.028; Std. β = -0.19, 95% CI [-0.36, -0.01]), indicating that individuals with aphantasia were more vulnerable to backward masking than controls.

INSERT FIGURE 5 HERE

### Reaction Time Analysis

We examined whether group differences extended to processing speed using the same linear mixed-effects model structure for reaction times (Figure 6). Stimulus duration showed no significant effect (β = -0.0004, 95% CI [-0.0008, 0.00005], t = -1.74, p = 0.082; Std. β = -0.05, 95% CI [-0.11, 0.006]), nor did backward masking (β = -0.00152, 95% CI [-0.06, 0.06], t(750) = -0.05, p = 0.959; Std. β = 0.08, 95% CI [-0.004, 0.16]). However, the aphantasia group showed significantly longer reaction times than controls (β = 0.22, 95% CI [0.07, 0.36], t = 2.86, p = 0.004; Std. β = 0.53, 95% CI [0.12, 0.93]), with no significant interactions.

INSERT FIGURE 6 HERE

### Speed-Accuracy Trade-off Control

Given that the aphantasia group showed higher accuracy but longer reaction times, we controlled for potential speed-accuracy trade-offs using the Balanced Integration Score (BIS; Liesefeld, Fu, & Zimmer, 2015; Liesefeld & Janczyk, 2019). The BIS combines speed and accuracy by taking the difference between their standardized scores and is less sensitive to speed-accuracy trade-offs than alternative measures (inverse efficiency score: Townsend & Ashby, 1983; rate-correct score: Woltz & Was, 2006; linear-integrated speed-accuracy score: Vandierendonck, 2017).

Using BIS as the outcome (Figure 7), stimulus duration remained significant (β = 0.00888, 95% CI [0.00683, 0.01], t = 8.52, p < .001; Std. β = 0.29, 95% CI [0.22, 0.35]), as did backward masking (β = -0.38, 95% CI [-0.64, -0.12], t(750) = -2.83, p = 0.005; Std. β = -0.27, 95% CI [-0.36, -0.17]). The main effect of group was no longer significant (β = -0.37, 95% CI [-0.92, 0.18], t(750) = -1.31, p = 0.191; Std. β = -0.29, 95% CI [-0.67, 0.08]), confirming that accuracy and reaction time differences reflected speed-accuracy trade-offs. Crucially, the group × masking interaction remained significant (β = -0.47, 95% CI [-0.86, -0.08], t(750) = -2.38, p = 0.018; Std. β = -0.10, 95% CI [-0.24, 0.04]), confirming greater masking vulnerability in aphantasia.

INSERT FIGURE 7 HERE

### VVIQ Correlations

Finally, we examined whether individual differences in VVIQ scores predicted categorization performance within each group using separate linear mixed-effects models with stimulus duration, backward masking, and VVIQ score as fixed effects and participant as a random effect. No significant main effects or interactions involving VVIQ emerged in either group, suggesting that within-group variation in self-reported imagery vividness did not predict task performance.

## Discussion

lines: Finally, we examined whether individual differences in VVIQ scores predicted categorization performance within each group using separate linear mixed-effects models with stimulus duration, backward masking, and VVIQ score as fixed effects and participant as a random effect. No significant main effects or interactions involving VVIQ emerged in either group, suggesting that within-group variation in self-reported imagery vividness did not predict task performance.
## Discussion

+We examined whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to backward masking during pattern completion. Two competing hypotheses emerged: first, that reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021) would render aphantasics more vulnerable to visual disruption; alternatively, that their reliance on semantic rather than sensory processing strategies (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Keogh et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023) might protect them from masking effects. Our findings supported the first hypothesis: while individuals with aphantasia successfully categorized partially occluded objects, they showed significantly greater impairment than controls when visual noise masks disrupted recurrent processing.

- Instructions to preserve ALL references and statistical details relevant to reproducibility

- Request to add note to CLAUDE.md about writing style preferences

The work involved systematic revision of an academic manuscript, with careful attention to scientific writing standards, statistical reporting, and journal requirements.

Summary:

   - Improve readability and writing quality while maintaining scientific rigor

   - Add figure placeholders for later insertion

   - Balanced Integration Score (BIS) for speed-accuracy trade-offs

   - Academic manuscript structure and formatting

4. Errors and fixes:

   - Fixed grammar: "two alternative hypothesis" → "two competing hypotheses"
   - User feedback: "don't remove details or reference though" - led to revision of analytical approach section to preserve all citations and technical details

5. Problem Solving:
   - Balanced conciseness with completeness as requested by user
   - Systematically organized manuscript with clear subsection headers
   - Standardized statistical reporting format while preserving all details
   - Created proper figure placeholders for later insertion
   - Maintained scientific rigor while improving readability

6. All user messages:

   - "let us move to results"

input.new_string:
We examined whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to backward masking during pattern completion. Two competing hypotheses emerged: first, that reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021) would render aphantasics more vulnerable to visual disruption; alternatively, that their reliance on semantic rather than sensory processing strategies (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Keogh et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023) might protect them from masking effects. Our findings supported the first hypothesis: while individuals with aphantasia successfully categorized partially occluded objects, they showed significantly greater impairment than controls when visual noise masks disrupted recurrent processing.

These findings suggest that aphantasics retain recurrent processing capabilities but in a fragile form that renders them more susceptible to visual interference. This interpretation aligns with neuroimaging evidence showing reduced—rather than absent—connectivity between frontoparietal executive cortices and visual cortices (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021) and between hippocampus and visual cortices (Monzel et al., 2023). This weakened recurrent connectivity likely underlies both their voluntary imagery deficits (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019) and their increased masking vulnerability. Recurrent communication between executive and visual regions supports pattern completion through predictive coding: higher-order regions identify features from partial sensory input, generate predictions that strengthen matching patterns in visual areas, and create prediction errors that refine representations iteratively (Den Ouden, Kok, & de Lange, 2012; Fyall et al., 2017; Shin et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2018). When these pathways are compromised, the resulting representations become fragile and vulnerable to disruption via backward masking.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Aphantasia and Visual Pattern Completion: Evidence for Fragile Recurrent Processing

## Abstract

Aphantasia is the inability to form voluntary mental images despite intact cognitive function. We hypothesized that aphantasia involves processing visual information at higher levels of abstraction, coupled with deficient recurrent feedback processes necessary for vivid imagery. To test whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to disruption of recurrent processing, we employed a perceptual pattern completion task with backward masking. Thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and 41 controls completed a task where they categorized briefly presented, partially occluded objects. Crucially, these images were followed by either visual noise masks (disrupting recurrent processing) or blank screens. Contrary to our initial hypothesis that aphantasics would be less affected by masking due to their reliance on semantic strategies, we found they were significantly more impacted by backward masking than controls. While both groups showed comparable accuracy without masking, the interaction between group and masking condition revealed greater vulnerability in aphantasics when recurrent processing was disrupted. After controlling for speed-accuracy trade-offs using the Balanced Integration Score, this interaction remained significant. These findings suggest that individuals with aphantasia possess intact but fragile recurrent processing, making them more susceptible to visual interference. This vulnerability may reflect the reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity previously observed in aphantasia, supporting the view that their imagery deficits stem from weakened top-down feedback mechanisms rather than their complete absence.

**Word count: 216**

## Keywords
aphantasia, mental imagery, recurrent processing, backward masking, pattern completion, visual perception, top-down feedback

## Introduction

Mental imagery—the ability to generate internal visual representations without external stimulation—plays a fundamental role in human cognition. This capacity to "see with the mind's eye" supports diverse cognitive functions including episodic memory (Greenberg & Knowlton, 2014), visual working memory (Albers, Kok, Toni, Dijkerman, & De Lange, 2013; Keogh & Pearson, 2011, 2014), and spatial navigation (Ghaem et al., 1997).

Despite its apparent importance, the functional role of mental imagery remains debated (Pearson & Kosslyn, 2015; Pylyshyn, 2003). Individual differences in imagery vividness, measured through trial-by-trial reports or the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ; Marks, 1973, 2023), provide insight into this relationship. Self-reported vividness correlates with multiple cognitive and neural measures: visual details in autobiographical memories (D'Argembeau & Van der Linden, 2006), neural reinstatement during recall (Barker, St-Laurent, & Buchsbaum, 2022; St-Laurent, Abdi, & Buchsbaum, 2015), and imagery-perception neural overlap (Albers et al., 2013; Bone et al., 2019; Cui, Jeter, Yang, Montague, & Eagleman, 2007; Lee, Kravitz, & Baker, 2012). VVIQ scores also predict imagery-induced priming in binocular rivalry (Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Pearson, Rademaker, & Tong, 2011) and pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay, Keogh, Andrillion, & Pearson, 2022). These converging findings demonstrate that subjective imagery reports reliably index objective neurocognitive processes.

Remarkably, some individuals report little to no mental imagery experience—a condition termed aphantasia (Zeman, Dewar, & Della Sala, 2015), first documented by Galton (1880). Prevalence estimates range from 1.9% (Beran, James, French, Haseltine, & Kleider-Offutt, 2023) to 3.9% (Dance, Ipser, & Simner, 2022). Individuals with aphantasia often discover their difference only when learning that others experience literal mental images, having previously interpreted "seeing in the mind's eye" as metaphorical (Keogh, Wicken, & Pearson, 2021; Zeman et al., 2015). Despite the apparent importance of imagery for cognition, these individuals lead typical lives, raising fundamental questions about how they accomplish imagery-dependent tasks.

Aphantasia's cognitive impact extends beyond subjective experience. Individuals with aphantasia report fewer episodic and visual details in autobiographical memories, particularly when simulating future events (Dawes, Keogh, Andrillon, & Pearson, 2020; Dawes, Keogh, Robuck, & Pearson, 2022; Milton et al., 2021; Zeman et al., 2020), linking this condition to severely deficient autobiographical memory (Palombo, Sheldon, & Levine, 2018). While many report dreams—suggesting possible unconscious imagery (Zeman et al., 2020)—these occur less frequently and with reduced vividness compared to controls (Dawes et al., 2020). However, objective measures argue against a simple metacognitive failure: aphantasics show neither imagery-based priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) nor pupillary responses to imagined brightness (Kay et al., 2022), indicating their representations genuinely lack sensory properties. This evidence points to a fundamental difference in cognitive strategy—aphantasics achieve comparable performance on visual working memory tasks by relying on semantic rather than sensory codes (Keogh et al., 2021).

Neuroimaging reveals intriguing paradoxes in aphantasia's neural basis. Despite their subjective absence of imagery, content-specific information can be decoded from early visual cortex during both imagery (Meng, Chang, Zhang, & Pearson, 2023) and working memory tasks (Weber, Christophel, Görgen, Soch, & Haynes, 2024) in individuals with aphantasia. However, crucial differences emerge: imagery representations cannot be cross-decoded with perceptual activity (Meng et al., 2023), and spatial imagery produces atypical lateralization—ipsilateral rather than the typical contralateral activation. These findings suggest that aphantasics' visual cortex maintains content-specific representations but encodes information in a fundamentally different format, likely stripped of sensory detail.

Beyond representational differences, aphantasia involves altered connectivity patterns. Resting-state and task-based neuroimaging reveal reduced connectivity between prefrontal and visual cortices, alongside diminished left anterior parietal activity during visualization attempts (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021). This pattern aligns with theoretical models positing that mental imagery requires stronger recurrent communication between frontoparietal executive regions and visual cortices than does perception alone (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019). The weakened connectivity likely explains the characteristic inability to voluntarily generate mental images in aphantasia.

These connectivity alterations extend to memory systems. During autobiographical memory retrieval, individuals with aphantasia show a paradoxical pattern: reduced hippocampal activation and hippocampal-visual connectivity, yet increased visual cortex activity (Monzel et al., 2023). This hyperactivity in visual regions during memory retrieval may reflect compensatory processes or, alternatively, a reduced signal-to-noise ratio that impairs the reinstatement of sensory details (Bergmann & Ortiz-Tudela, 2023; Monzel et al., 2023). Such findings further support the view that aphantasia involves disrupted communication between memory and sensory systems.

Given these widespread connectivity disruptions, we investigated whether perceptual pattern completion—another recurrent-dependent process (Rolls, 2013)—is compromised in aphantasia. Pattern completion allows recognition of partially occluded objects through recurrent connections that spread activation from visible features to missing ones (Fyall, El-Shamayleh, Choi, Shea-Brown, & Pasupathy, 2017; Rolls, 2013), a mechanism supported by computational modeling (Tang et al., 2018). Critically, this process can be selectively disrupted using backward masking, where visual noise presented shortly after a target stimulus interferes with recurrent processing (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000; Lollo et al., 2000; Rolls, Tovée, & Panzeri, 1999; Tang et al., 2018; Wyatte, Curran, & O'Reilly, 2012). The mechanism involves temporal conflict: feedforward signals from the mask ascend the visual hierarchy simultaneously with descending feedback about the target, disrupting the iterative refinement necessary for pattern completion (Lamme & Roelfsema, 2000).

This paradigm offers a unique window into aphantasia's perceptual consequences. Two competing hypotheses emerge from the literature. First, given their reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Milton et al., 2021), individuals with aphantasia might show greater vulnerability to backward masking, struggling more than controls when recurrent processing is disrupted. Alternatively, their reliance on semantic rather than sensory strategies (Keogh et al., 2021) and representations lacking sensory detail (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023) might render them less dependent on recurrent processing, thus paradoxically protecting them from masking effects. To test these hypotheses, we presented thirty-four individuals with aphantasia and matched controls with briefly displayed, partially occluded objects for categorization. Critical trials included backward masks designed to disrupt the recurrent processing necessary for pattern completion, allowing us to assess whether aphantasia involves fundamentally altered perceptual mechanisms.

## Methods

### Participants

Thirty-nine individuals with aphantasia (aged 18-77) participated: 35 self-identified through Reddit and Facebook communities, plus 4 identified via VVIQ scores during control recruitment. Inclusion criteria required lifelong absence of voluntary mental imagery with no precipitating injury. Four participants were excluded for excessive non-responding or chance-level performance, yielding a final aphantasia sample of 35. The control group comprised 41 age-matched individuals (aged 18-77) recruited via Prolific (n=38), Reddit (n=2), and Facebook (n=1). All participants provided informed consent and received compensation for their time.

### Measures

Participants completed the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire 2 (VVIQ2; Marks, 1973), except for 17 controls who did not complete this measure. The VVIQ2 comprises 32 items across eight scenarios (e.g., driving, beach scenes) rated on a 5-point scale from 1 ("No image at all, you only 'know' that you are thinking of the object") to 5 ("Perfectly clear and as vivid as normal vision"). Lower scores indicate reduced imagery vividness.

### Stimuli

We used 320 grayscale images of objects from four categories (clothing, food, toys, and tools; see Figure 1). Images were sourced from Google image searches and resized to a maximum of 450 pixels (height or width) while preserving aspect ratios.

INSERT FIGURE 1 HERE

### Procedure

Participants completed a backward masking task (320 trials across two blocks) adapted from Tang et al. (2018; see Figure 2). Each trial followed a fixed sequence: 500 ms fixation, target image presentation (25, 50, 75, 100, or 150 ms), followed by either a visual noise mask or blank screen (500 ms), then a categorization response screen. Participants categorized objects into four groups (clothing, food, tools, toys) using keyboard responses 1-4.

INSERT FIGURE 2 HERE

Two image conditions were used: 25% intact images and 75% partially occluded images revealed through six Gaussian bubbles (σ = 26 pixels; Gosselin & Schyns, 2001). Masking occurred on 50% of trials (noise mask vs. blank screen). The design yielded 40 exemplars per category per block, with eight stimuli per duration level, counterbalanced across masking conditions.

## Results

### VVIQ Validation

We first verified that our groups differed in self-reported imagery vividness. VVIQ scores showed a bimodal distribution (Figure 3) with overall M = 73.53 (SD = 43.14, range = 32-160). Hartigan's dip test confirmed multimodality (Dip = 0.096, p < 0.001). The aphantasia group (M = 43.31, SD = 21.30, range = 32-90) scored significantly lower than controls (M = 119.52, SD = 21.45, range = 96-160), t(47) = 13.30, p < .0001, Cohen's d = 3.57.

INSERT FIGURE 3 HERE

### Categorization Performance

We examined whether backward masking differentially affected categorization performance between groups using linear mixed-effects models in R (lme4 package; Bates, Mächler, Bolker, & Walker, 2015) with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom approximation (lmerTest package; Kuznetsova, Brockhoff, & Christensen, 2017). Models included stimulus duration, masking condition, and group as fixed effects, with participant as a random effect. Separate analyses were conducted for intact and partially occluded images.

#### Intact Images

For unoccluded images (Figure 4), stimulus duration showed no significant effect (β = 0.00109, 95% CI [-0.00164, 0.00381], t = 0.78, p = 0.434; Std. β = 0.04, 95% CI [-0.06, 0.13]), with comparable performance between groups (β = -0.43, 95% CI [-0.96, 0.10], t = -1.60, p = 0.110; Std. β = -0.30, 95% CI [-0.65, 0.05]). Backward masking significantly reduced accuracy (β = -0.49, 95% CI [-0.84, -0.14], t = -2.76, p = 0.006; Std. β = -0.09, 95% CI [-0.22, 0.05]).

INSERT FIGURE 4 HERE

#### Partially Occluded Images

For partially occluded images (Figure 5), accuracy increased with longer stimulus durations (β = 0.00172, 95% CI [0.00140, 0.00204], t = 10.54, p < .001; Std. β = 0.45, 95% CI [0.37, 0.54]). Backward masking significantly reduced accuracy (β = -0.09, 95% CI [-0.13, -0.04], t = -4.09, p < .001; Std. β = -0.38, 95% CI [-0.50, -0.26]). The aphantasia group showed higher overall accuracy (β = 0.06, 95% CI [0.0008, 0.12], t = 1.99, p = 0.047; Std. β = 0.19, 95% CI [-0.10, 0.49]). Critically, a significant group × masking interaction emerged (β = -0.07, 95% CI [-0.13, -0.008], t = -2.21, p = 0.028; Std. β = -0.19, 95% CI [-0.36, -0.01]), indicating that individuals with aphantasia were more vulnerable to backward masking than controls.

INSERT FIGURE 5 HERE

### Reaction Time Analysis

We examined whether group differences extended to processing speed using the same linear mixed-effects model structure for reaction times (Figure 6). Stimulus duration showed no significant effect (β = -0.0004, 95% CI [-0.0008, 0.00005], t = -1.74, p = 0.082; Std. β = -0.05, 95% CI [-0.11, 0.006]), nor did backward masking (β = -0.00152, 95% CI [-0.06, 0.06], t(750) = -0.05, p = 0.959; Std. β = 0.08, 95% CI [-0.004, 0.16]). However, the aphantasia group showed significantly longer reaction times than controls (β = 0.22, 95% CI [0.07, 0.36], t = 2.86, p = 0.004; Std. β = 0.53, 95% CI [0.12, 0.93]), with no significant interactions.

INSERT FIGURE 6 HERE

### Speed-Accuracy Trade-off Control

Given that the aphantasia group showed higher accuracy but longer reaction times, we controlled for potential speed-accuracy trade-offs using the Balanced Integration Score (BIS; Liesefeld, Fu, & Zimmer, 2015; Liesefeld & Janczyk, 2019). The BIS combines speed and accuracy by taking the difference between their standardized scores and is less sensitive to speed-accuracy trade-offs than alternative measures (inverse efficiency score: Townsend & Ashby, 1983; rate-correct score: Woltz & Was, 2006; linear-integrated speed-accuracy score: Vandierendonck, 2017).

Using BIS as the outcome (Figure 7), stimulus duration remained significant (β = 0.00888, 95% CI [0.00683, 0.01], t = 8.52, p < .001; Std. β = 0.29, 95% CI [0.22, 0.35]), as did backward masking (β = -0.38, 95% CI [-0.64, -0.12], t(750) = -2.83, p = 0.005; Std. β = -0.27, 95% CI [-0.36, -0.17]). The main effect of group was no longer significant (β = -0.37, 95% CI [-0.92, 0.18], t(750) = -1.31, p = 0.191; Std. β = -0.29, 95% CI [-0.67, 0.08]), confirming that accuracy and reaction time differences reflected speed-accuracy trade-offs. Crucially, the group × masking interaction remained significant (β = -0.47, 95% CI [-0.86, -0.08], t(750) = -2.38, p = 0.018; Std. β = -0.10, 95% CI [-0.24, 0.04]), confirming greater masking vulnerability in aphantasia.

INSERT FIGURE 7 HERE

### VVIQ Correlations

Finally, we examined whether individual differences in VVIQ scores predicted categorization performance within each group using separate linear mixed-effects models with stimulus duration, backward masking, and VVIQ score as fixed effects and participant as a random effect. No significant main effects or interactions involving VVIQ emerged in either group, suggesting that within-group variation in self-reported imagery vividness did not predict task performance.

## Discussion

We examined whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to backward masking during pattern completion. Two competing hypotheses emerged: first, that reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021) would render aphantasics more vulnerable to visual disruption; alternatively, that their reliance on semantic rather than sensory processing strategies (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Keogh et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023) might protect them from masking effects. Our findings supported the first hypothesis: while individuals with aphantasia successfully categorized partially occluded objects, they showed significantly greater impairment than controls when visual noise masks disrupted recurrent processing.

lines: ## Discussion

We examined whether individuals with aphantasia show differential vulnerability to backward masking during pattern completion. Two competing hypotheses emerged: first, that reduced frontoparietal-visual connectivity (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023; Milton et al., 2021) would render aphantasics more vulnerable to visual disruption; alternatively, that their reliance on semantic rather than sensory processing strategies (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Keogh et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2023) might protect them from masking effects. Our findings supported the first hypothesis: while individuals with aphantasia successfully categorized partially occluded objects, they showed significantly greater impairment than controls when visual noise masks disrupted recurrent processing.

+These findings suggest that aphantasics retain recurrent processing capabilities but in a fragile form that renders them more susceptible to visual interference. This interpretation aligns with neuroimaging evidence showing reduced—rather than absent—connectivity between frontoparietal executive cortices and visual cortices (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021) and between hippocampus and visual cortices (Monzel et al., 2023). This weakened recurrent connectivity likely underlies both their voluntary imagery deficits (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019) and their increased masking vulnerability. Recurrent communication between executive and visual regions supports pattern completion through predictive coding: higher-order regions identify features from partial sensory input, generate predictions that strengthen matching patterns in visual areas, and create prediction errors that refine representations iteratively (Den Ouden, Kok, & de Lange, 2012; Fyall et al., 2017; Shin et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2018). When these pathways are compromised, the resulting representations become fragile and vulnerable to disruption via backward masking.

content.content:
41→Mental imagery, as defined in a review by Pearson and colleagues (Pearson, Naselaris, Holmes, & Kosslyn, 2015), is the representation of sensory information without a direct external stimulus. Under this definition, the retrieval and representation of missing portions of an occluded image via pattern completion can be considered a form of mental imagery, as the represented missing portions are not present in the external stimulus. But given the evidence that the imagery of aphantasics lacks sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023), how is it that aphantasics are able to complete the task? One possibility is that aphantasics have a form of intact unconscious mental imagery that occurs outside their awareness or control. In support of this idea, some aphantasics report having vivid sleeping dreams (Zeman et al., 2020), though use of the term “unconscious” in this manner is debated due to the phenomenological nature of dreams (see Nanay, 2021). Thus, while aphantasics may not be able to voluntarily evoke an image of a beach, top-down visual processes that occur outside of conscious awareness of may still be intact. It should be noted that not all individuals report having dreams, with some indicating they do not experience mental imagery at all, conscious or unconscious. Although, it is be possible that the majority of the aphantasics in the current study had intact unconscious mental imagery, it is not currently clear how to reliably measure this construct in a way that clearly dissociates from conscious imagery.
    42→One thing to note about our results is the lack of within group relationship between VVIQ scores and task performance (categorization of partial images). Given that the VVIQ is thought to measure individual differences in imagery abilities, we would expect that variance in VVIQ scores should correlate with task performance and recurrent processing capability in our control group. Thus, our results raise questions about the construct validity of the VVIQ. Admittedly, our inability to detect a relationship between VVIQ scores and categorization performance may reflect a lack of statistical power; however, concerns over the mixed results on the validity of the VVIQ have been previously raised (Chara & Hamm, 1989) with a recent meta-analysis demonstrating that use of trial-by-trial vividness reports provide higher predictive value than VVIQ scores (Runge, Bakhilau, Omer, & D’Angiulli, 2015). A related issue is that there is little variation in the VVIQ scores of our sample of Aphantasics and samples of other studies. This is partly by design as researchers commonly use a narrow range of scores to diagnose or verify individuals with aphantasia (a range of 16-32 is often used for the VVIQ or 32-64 for the VVIQ2). Despite this, recent surveys suggest that there is a spectrum of imagery experience amongst those with Aphantasia (Zeman et al., 2020).  The limited variation in VVIQ scores among aphantasics may reduce our ability to detect their individual differences in imaging abilities. For example, VVIQ scores predict the degree to which mental imagery affects visual priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) and the pupillary light response (Kay et al., 2022) in control participants. Meanwhile, there is no relationship between VVIQ scores and these behavioral measures in aphantasics. Similarly, we find no relationship between VVIQ scores and categorization performance in our Aphantasic group. Together these results suggest that additional tools or measures are needed to help examine individual differences in imagery in aphantasia.
    43→ A related concern is the heterogeneous nature of aphantasia. With the accumulation of anecdotal and experimental evidence, it is becoming apparent that there exists a wide spectrum of experience with mental imagery among those with aphantasia. Aphantasia does not only describe a complete absence of mental imagery, but also includes those with dim or vague imagery. As mentioned earlier there are individual differences in the ability to dream, and among those who are able to dream, the quality of those dreams varies as well. For some individuals dreams occur infrequently and are visually impoverished compared to controls (Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020; Milton et al., 2021), while others report experiencing highly vivid dreams (Zeman et al., 2020). Recent reports find that individuals with aphantasia tend to be affected in multiple modalities (Dance, Ward, & Simner, 2021; Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020; Alexei Joel Dawes, Keogh, & Pearson, 2023) with 97% of the surveyed sample reporting similar weak imagery in at least one other non-imagery modality, and 62% reporting weak (absent or vague/dim) imagery in all modalities (tactile, gustatory, auditory, bodily, olfactory, movement). While the multimodal nature of aphantasia may not directly speak to the current study, it does suggest a direction of future research on aphantasia as a whole. For example, if recurrent connectivity during mental imagery is generally deficient in aphantasics, the kind of effect we observed in the present study should be reproduced in other modalities. Existing studies have utilized the VVIQ to gauge mental imagery, which primarily measures vividness in the visual domain. Thus, future studies would benefit from expanding scale ranges or developing paradigms that allow for a wider range of imagery experience to be considered, including those in other modalities.
    44→In conclusion, aphantasia describes the condition in which one is unable to voluntarily form vivid mental images. These deficits in imagery are thought to be related to a reduced ability to reinstate perceptual activity in early visual regions via top-down mediation. Supporting this idea, aphantasics have been shown to haveweaker connectivity between frontoparietal executive and visual cortices.  Pattern completion of partial objects is also thought to rely on recurrent connectivity between these regions and this recurrent processing can be probed with the rapid presentation of a backward mask. Here we asked if pattern completion in aphantasics would be differentially impacted by backward masking with visual noise. We had two alternative hypothesis predicting that the ability of aphantasics to categorize partial images would be relatively undisrupted due to their preference for semantic strategies or that they would be more vulnerable to visual disruption due to their weaker recurrent connectivity. Our findings were in line with our second hypothesis, aphantasics were more negatively impacted by visual noise than controls. Our findings suggests that aphantasics have weak, but nevertheless, intact recurrent processing that is more vulnerable to visual interference. 
    45→

file.content:
Mental imagery, as defined in a review by Pearson and colleagues (Pearson, Naselaris, Holmes, & Kosslyn, 2015), is the representation of sensory information without a direct external stimulus. Under this definition, the retrieval and representation of missing portions of an occluded image via pattern completion can be considered a form of mental imagery, as the represented missing portions are not present in the external stimulus. But given the evidence that the imagery of aphantasics lacks sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023), how is it that aphantasics are able to complete the task? One possibility is that aphantasics have a form of intact unconscious mental imagery that occurs outside their awareness or control. In support of this idea, some aphantasics report having vivid sleeping dreams (Zeman et al., 2020), though use of the term “unconscious” in this manner is debated due to the phenomenological nature of dreams (see Nanay, 2021). Thus, while aphantasics may not be able to voluntarily evoke an image of a beach, top-down visual processes that occur outside of conscious awareness of may still be intact. It should be noted that not all individuals report having dreams, with some indicating they do not experience mental imagery at all, conscious or unconscious. Although, it is be possible that the majority of the aphantasics in the current study had intact unconscious mental imagery, it is not currently clear how to reliably measure this construct in a way that clearly dissociates from conscious imagery.
One thing to note about our results is the lack of within group relationship between VVIQ scores and task performance (categorization of partial images). Given that the VVIQ is thought to measure individual differences in imagery abilities, we would expect that variance in VVIQ scores should correlate with task performance and recurrent processing capability in our control group. Thus, our results raise questions about the construct validity of the VVIQ. Admittedly, our inability to detect a relationship between VVIQ scores and categorization performance may reflect a lack of statistical power; however, concerns over the mixed results on the validity of the VVIQ have been previously raised (Chara & Hamm, 1989) with a recent meta-analysis demonstrating that use of trial-by-trial vividness reports provide higher predictive value than VVIQ scores (Runge, Bakhilau, Omer, & D’Angiulli, 2015). A related issue is that there is little variation in the VVIQ scores of our sample of Aphantasics and samples of other studies. This is partly by design as researchers commonly use a narrow range of scores to diagnose or verify individuals with aphantasia (a range of 16-32 is often used for the VVIQ or 32-64 for the VVIQ2). Despite this, recent surveys suggest that there is a spectrum of imagery experience amongst those with Aphantasia (Zeman et al., 2020).  The limited variation in VVIQ scores among aphantasics may reduce our ability to detect their individual differences in imaging abilities. For example, VVIQ scores predict the degree to which mental imagery affects visual priming (Keogh & Pearson, 2018) and the pupillary light response (Kay et al., 2022) in control participants. Meanwhile, there is no relationship between VVIQ scores and these behavioral measures in aphantasics. Similarly, we find no relationship between VVIQ scores and categorization performance in our Aphantasic group. Together these results suggest that additional tools or measures are needed to help examine individual differences in imagery in aphantasia.
 A related concern is the heterogeneous nature of aphantasia. With the accumulation of anecdotal and experimental evidence, it is becoming apparent that there exists a wide spectrum of experience with mental imagery among those with aphantasia. Aphantasia does not only describe a complete absence of mental imagery, but also includes those with dim or vague imagery. As mentioned earlier there are individual differences in the ability to dream, and among those who are able to dream, the quality of those dreams varies as well. For some individuals dreams occur infrequently and are visually impoverished compared to controls (Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020; Milton et al., 2021), while others report experiencing highly vivid dreams (Zeman et al., 2020). Recent reports find that individuals with aphantasia tend to be affected in multiple modalities (Dance, Ward, & Simner, 2021; Alexei J. Dawes et al., 2020; Alexei Joel Dawes, Keogh, & Pearson, 2023) with 97% of the surveyed sample reporting similar weak imagery in at least one other non-imagery modality, and 62% reporting weak (absent or vague/dim) imagery in all modalities (tactile, gustatory, auditory, bodily, olfactory, movement). While the multimodal nature of aphantasia may not directly speak to the current study, it does suggest a direction of future research on aphantasia as a whole. For example, if recurrent connectivity during mental imagery is generally deficient in aphantasics, the kind of effect we observed in the present study should be reproduced in other modalities. Existing studies have utilized the VVIQ to gauge mental imagery, which primarily measures vividness in the visual domain. Thus, future studies would benefit from expanding scale ranges or developing paradigms that allow for a wider range of imagery experience to be considered, including those in other modalities.
In conclusion, aphantasia describes the condition in which one is unable to voluntarily form vivid mental images. These deficits in imagery are thought to be related to a reduced ability to reinstate perceptual activity in early visual regions via top-down mediation. Supporting this idea, aphantasics have been shown to haveweaker connectivity between frontoparietal executive and visual cortices.  Pattern completion of partial objects is also thought to rely on recurrent connectivity between these regions and this recurrent processing can be probed with the rapid presentation of a backward mask. Here we asked if pattern completion in aphantasics would be differentially impacted by backward masking with visual noise. We had two alternative hypothesis predicting that the ability of aphantasics to categorize partial images would be relatively undisrupted due to their preference for semantic strategies or that they would be more vulnerable to visual disruption due to their weaker recurrent connectivity. Our findings were in line with our second hypothesis, aphantasics were more negatively impacted by visual noise than controls. Our findings suggests that aphantasics have weak, but nevertheless, intact recurrent processing that is more vulnerable to visual interference.

content.text: Now I'll work on the next paragraph about unconscious mental imagery and pattern completion (line 41). This paragraph discusses how aphantasics might accomplish the task despite lacking conscious imagery.

input.old_string: These findings suggest that aphantasics retain recurrent processing capabilities but in a fragile form that renders them more susceptible to visual interference. This interpretation aligns with neuroimaging evidence showing reduced—rather than absent—connectivity between frontoparietal executive cortices and visual cortices (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021) and between hippocampus and visual cortices (Monzel et al., 2023). This weakened recurrent connectivity likely underlies both their voluntary imagery deficits (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019) and their increased masking vulnerability. Recurrent communication between executive and visual regions supports pattern completion through predictive coding: higher-order regions identify features from partial sensory input, generate predictions that strengthen matching patterns in visual areas, and create prediction errors that refine representations iteratively (Den Ouden, Kok, & de Lange, 2012; Fyall et al., 2017; Shin et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2018). When these pathways are compromised, the resulting representations become fragile and vulnerable to disruption via backward masking.
input.new_string:
These findings suggest that aphantasics retain recurrent processing capabilities but in a fragile form that renders them more susceptible to visual interference. This interpretation aligns with neuroimaging evidence showing reduced—rather than absent—connectivity between frontoparietal executive cortices and visual cortices (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021) and between hippocampus and visual cortices (Monzel et al., 2023). This weakened recurrent connectivity likely underlies both their voluntary imagery deficits (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019) and their increased masking vulnerability. Recurrent communication between executive and visual regions supports pattern completion through predictive coding: higher-order regions identify features from partial sensory input, generate predictions that strengthen matching patterns in visual areas, and create prediction errors that refine representations iteratively (Den Ouden, Kok, & de Lange, 2012; Fyall et al., 2017; Shin et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2018). When these pathways are compromised, the resulting representations become fragile and vulnerable to disruption via backward masking.

A critical question emerges: given that aphantasics' imagery lacks sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023), how do they complete pattern completion tasks? Pattern completion requires representing missing image portions not present in the external stimulus—a form of mental imagery by Pearson et al.'s (2015) definition. One possibility is intact unconscious imagery operating outside conscious awareness. Supporting this, some aphantasics report vivid dreams (Zeman et al., 2020), though the "unconscious" designation remains debated given dreams' phenomenological nature (Nanay, 2021). While aphantasics cannot voluntarily evoke beach imagery, top-down visual processes may function unconsciously. However, not all aphantasics report dreams, and reliable measurement distinguishing conscious from unconscious imagery remains challenging.

These findings suggest that aphantasics retain recurrent processing capabilities but in a fragile form that renders them more susceptible to visual interference. This interpretation aligns with neuroimaging evidence showing reduced—rather than absent—connectivity between frontoparietal executive cortices and visual cortices (Liu, Cohen, & Bartolomeo, 2023; Milton et al., 2021) and between hippocampus and visual cortices (Monzel et al., 2023). This weakened recurrent connectivity likely underlies both their voluntary imagery deficits (Dijkstra, Bosch, & van Gerven, 2019) and their increased masking vulnerability. Recurrent communication between executive and visual regions supports pattern completion through predictive coding: higher-order regions identify features from partial sensory input, generate predictions that strengthen matching patterns in visual areas, and create prediction errors that refine representations iteratively (Den Ouden, Kok, & de Lange, 2012; Fyall et al., 2017; Shin et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2018). When these pathways are compromised, the resulting representations become fragile and vulnerable to disruption via backward masking.

A critical question emerges: given that aphantasics' imagery lacks sensory and phenomenal properties (Kay et al., 2022; Keogh & Pearson, 2018; Meng et al., 2023), how do they complete pattern completion tasks? Pattern completion requires representing missing image portions not present in the external stimulus—a form of mental imagery by Pearson et al.'s (2015) definition. One possibility is intact unconscious imagery operating outside conscious awareness. Supporting this, some aphantasics report vivid dreams (Zeman et al., 2020), though the "unconscious" designation remains debated given dreams' phenomenological nature (Nanay, 2021). While aphantasics cannot voluntarily evoke beach imagery, top-down visual processes may function unconsciously. However, not all aphantasics report dreams, and reliable measurement distinguishing conscious from unconscious imagery remains challenging.
